> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (717 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
> **Age 19 (718 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
> **Age 20 (719 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
> **Age 21 (720 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.
narrative: **Age 18 (717 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 19 (718 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.

**Age 20 (719 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 21 (720 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.

> **Age 19 (716 TR)** — The journey required more of her than she had expected and gave back more than she had hoped.
> **Age 20 (717 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (718 TR)** — She bore a child. Vala Carter came into the world healthy. week 40 Vala Carter born (female)
> **Age 22 (719 TR)** — She was given in marriage, binding two households together. Married Rhoric Butcher (artisan, age 26)
> **Age 22 (719 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
> **Age 23 (720 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.

He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
> **Age 21 (717 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
> **Age 22 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 23 (719 TR)** — He took a wrong turn and came out somewhere he had never been. The village had a good bakehouse and the wrong road became one he always took.
> **Age 24 (720 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (713 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
> **Age 19 (714 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Arith (clergy)
> **Age 20 (715 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (716 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
> **Age 22 (717 TR)** — She borrowed something meaning to return it. That was years ago. It has become hers through inertia.
> **Age 23 (718 TR)** — She passed a room in the night and saw light underneath the door. She paused and then went on. She asked about it in the morning.
> **Age 24 (719 TR)** — Dancing was expected of her from childhood. She was accomplished.
> **Age 25 (720 TR)** — She saw something in the grain of a beam — a face, a figure, a landscape — and noted it and went on.

> **Age 26 (720 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (711 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger.
> **Age 19 (712 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.
> **Age 20 (713 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (714 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
> **Age 22 (715 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; she left someone badly hurt.
> **Age 23 (716 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
> **Age 24 (717 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
> **Age 25 (718 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.
> **Age 26 (719 TR)** — The party arrived in the evening — four men, armed, carrying themselves like soldiers but wearing nothing identifying. She gave them the south room with two beds and told the kitchen to send up hot food. She has been running this inn for twelve years and she knows exactly what these men are and what they are doing in her inn, which is transiting from somewhere to somewhere on business that is not her business, and the correct response to that is: good rooms, good food, prompt service, correct change, and a very accurate memory of faces.
> **Age 27 (720 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Roric Warson died

> **Age 21 (713 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. > [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (709 TR)** — She watched the web of favours and enmities carefully, learning where to place her feet.
> **Age 19 (710 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
> **Age 20 (711 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Trovan Brightcoin (merchant, age 24)
> **Age 20 (711 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.
> **Age 21 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 21 (712 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
> **Age 22 (713 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
> **Age 22 (713 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
> **Age 23 (714 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
> **Age 23 (714 TR)** — She bore a child. Geira Brightcoin came into the world healthy. week 40 Geira Brightcoin born (female)
> **Age 24 (715 TR)** — The inn was so terrible she slept sitting up and was grateful for the experience as a story if nothing else.
> **Age 24 (715 TR)** — She lost the child early — before she had said much aloud about it. She said nothing after, either. week 9
> **Age 25 (716 TR)** — The cat left a mouse on her pillow. She accepted this as the highest compliment.
> **Age 26 (717 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
> **Age 26 (717 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
> **Age 27 (718 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with.
> **Age 28 (719 TR)** — She ducked into a temple to get out of the rain and stayed far longer than the rain lasted.
> **Age 28 (719 TR)** — She bore a child. Noral Brightcoin came into the world healthy. week 40 Noral Brightcoin born (male)
> **Age 29 (720 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness passed without lasting harm.
> **Age 29 (720 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (708 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Marath Butcher (artisan, age 16)
> **Age 19 (709 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.
> **Age 19 (709 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Edris Edsen — survived. week 40 Edris Edsen born (female)
> **Age 19 (709 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Edris Edsen died (age 0)
> **Age 20 (710 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
> **Age 20 (710 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 7
> **Age 21 (711 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
> **Age 21 (711 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — lame Arith Edsen born (female, lame)
> **Age 22 (712 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
> **Age 22 (712 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
> **Age 23 (713 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
> **Age 23 (713 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
> **Age 24 (714 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
> **Age 24 (714 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Beren Edsen. week 40 Beren Edsen born (male)
> **Age 25 (715 TR)** — He told a story at a campfire that the others repeated for years. He had mostly made it up.
> **Age 25 (715 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
> **Age 26 (716 TR)** — He reached the temple doors ahead of the men pursuing him and the priest let him in. Whether it was the law or simply that the priest had seen his face and made a judgment, he was never certain. He stayed three days on a pallet in the vestibule and left by the back when they told him it was clear.
> **Age 26 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 27 (717 TR)** — Steel found him. The wound healed, after a fashion.
> **Age 27 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 27 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 28 (718 TR)** — His reliability and competence were noticed. Rank followed.
> **Age 28 (718 TR)** — He lay awake thinking about what would happen to the child after he was gone. There was no answer he could land on.
> **Age 29 (719 TR)** — The injury was too deep. He would never move as freely again. A grievous wound to the shoulder. The damage was permanent — strength reduced by 3.
> **Age 30 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 30 (720 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (707 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
> **Age 19 (708 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively.
> **Age 20 (709 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
> **Age 21 (710 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Branna Branson died
> **Age 22 (711 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 35
> **Age 23 (712 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone.
> **Age 24 (713 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 25 (714 TR)** — She kept to it when most would have stopped. The discipline became its own reward.
> **Age 26 (715 TR)** — She opened what she thought was the right door. It was not. She apologised to the people inside and withdrew.
> **Age 27 (716 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.
> **Age 28 (717 TR)** — She was not devout. She went to the Matakea the night before the assault because several people she respected had done the same, and because the mathematics of the next day were not reassuring. She did not find God in the conversation. She found something else — a clarity about what she was willing to die for, which turned out to be enough.
> **Age 29 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 30 (719 TR)** — The Trail was Pagaelin territory in every practical sense regardless of what the maps said. She learned its rhythms — which stretches drew attention, which seasons were dangerous, which small gestures communicated respect without communicating weakness.
> **Age 31 (720 TR)** — She saw a situation developing that she might have done something about. She walked on. The question of whether she was right to has not fully settled.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (706 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
> **Age 19 (707 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Alura Halsen (peasant, age 17)
> **Age 19 (707 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 15
> **Age 20 (708 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
> **Age 20 (708 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 32
> **Age 21 (709 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
> **Age 21 (709 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
> **Age 22 (710 TR)** — Two years underground made him a freeman. The guild gave him a tin medallion as legal proof, which he wore for a decade before it was stolen and which he has spent twenty years wishing he still had. His former lord sent someone to contest the freedom in the first year. The mine moot ruled for him. He did not return to his home village. He has not been back in thirty years and thinks about it occasionally.
> **Age 23 (711 TR)** — The franchise was available — a small workshop in the wool quarter, nothing spectacular but solid — and he turned it down. He told the guild officer he wanted another two years of journeyman work first. The guild officer wrote this down without visible opinion. The other journeymen had opinions, most of them unflattering. He is aware that he is making a decision most people in his position do not make and he is making it for reasons he cannot fully articulate, which is perhaps the real reason: he is not yet sure he is as good as he needs to be, and he would rather know before he has a workshop depending on him.
> **Age 24 (712 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
> **Age 24 (712 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
> **Age 25 (713 TR)** — The piece is done. He has done everything he knows how to do to it. Now it sits on the bench and he waits for the master to look at it and say something, which the master has not yet done because the master is finishing something else. This is a large fraction of an apprentice's life: doing the work, then waiting to find out what the work meant. He is getting better at the waiting. He uses the time to look at the piece from different angles and find the things the master will find before the master finds them.
> **Age 25 (713 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
> **Age 26 (714 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
> **Age 26 (714 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 9
> **Age 27 (715 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
> **Age 27 (715 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Yara Coring born (female)
> **Age 28 (716 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
> **Age 28 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 29 (717 TR)** — He made something — a wooden figure, a knotted rope, a small carving — with no purpose in mind. He gave it away.
> **Age 29 (717 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
> **Age 30 (718 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
> **Age 31 (719 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
> **Age 32 (720 TR)** — There is a journeyman three years ahead of him who considers the apprentices his personal labour reserve. This is not unusual — the structure permits it — but the specific use he makes of it has crossed into something the master would probably not endorse if he were paying attention. He has made the calculation: confronting the journeyman directly produces a worse outcome than working within the structure while quietly ensuring the master sees the results of the arrangement. He is learning that craft skill and workplace politics are not the same skill and that both are necessary.
> **Age 32 (720 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — lame Dalla Coring born (female, lame)
> **Age 32 (720 TR)** — Dalla Coring died — the lame child he had worried over for years. Dalla Coring died (age 0)
> **Age 22** *(class change)* — Mine service earned legal freedom; entered guild as apprentice [peasant → artisan]

This is not unusual — the structure permits it — but the specific use he makes of it has crossed into something the master would probably not endorse if he were paying attention. He has made the calculation: confronting the journeyman directly produces a worse outcome than working within the structure while quietly ensuring the master sees the results of the arrangement. He is learning that craft skill and workplace politics are not the same skill and that both are necessary.

**Age 32 (720 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — lame Dalla Coring born (female, lame)
**Age 32 (720 TR)** — Dalla Coring died — the lame child he had worried over for years. Dalla Coring died (age 0)

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (705 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
> **Age 19 (706 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.
> **Age 20 (707 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (708 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
> **Age 22 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 23 (710 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
> **Age 24 (711 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
> **Age 25 (712 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
> **Age 26 (713 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Elric Chelkar
> **Age 27 (714 TR)** — A man of means noticed her. What followed was not love, precisely, but it was not nothing either. She had a room that was warm and food that was regular, and she understood the terms.
> **Age 28 (715 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Kelan Weaver (artisan)
> **Age 29 (716 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands.
> **Age 30 (717 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.
> **Age 31 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 32 (719 TR)** — He made it legitimate. She had not let herself believe he would. The wedding was modest and the gossip was fierce and she did not care about either.
> **Age 33 (720 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Halden Glazier. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Halden Glazier born (male)
> **Age 32** *(class change)* — Married above her station [artisan → merchant]

He sells at markets and from a small workshop shelf. The Guild of Arcane Lore has never bothered to classify what he does; the Woodcrafters' Guild has decided it is beneath their notice. He is therefore free to operate without guild interference, which he considers the main advantage of his occupation. His work is good. > [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (704 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
> **Age 19 (705 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
> **Age 20 (706 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
> **Age 21 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 22 (708 TR)** — He heard a song at a market and could not stop hearing it for three months. He did not know the words.
> **Age 23 (709 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 24 (710 TR)** — A crow appeared every morning for a month on the same post. He began to look for it. One day it was gone.
> **Age 25 (711 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Vala Alendyl (noble)
> **Age 26 (712 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
> **Age 27 (713 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
> **Age 28 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 29 (715 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
> **Age 30 (716 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
> **Age 31 (717 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
> **Age 32 (718 TR)** — He drank more than he intended at a gathering and said things he would not have said sober. He reviewed these the next day at length.
> **Age 33 (719 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
> **Age 34 (720 TR)** — It had not started as enmity. By the end, it was nothing else. Vala Alendyl became an enemy
narrative: **Age 18 (704 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 19 (705 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 20 (706 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 21 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 22 (708 TR)** — He heard a song at a market and could not stop hearing it for three months. He did not know the words.
**Age 23 (709 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 24 (710 TR)** — A crow appeared every morning for a month on the same post. He began to look for it. One day it was gone.
**Age 25 (711 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Vala Alendyl (noble)
**Age 26 (712 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 27 (713 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
**Age 28 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 29 (715 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.

**Age 30 (716 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
**Age 31 (717 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
**Age 32 (718 TR)** — He drank more than he intended at a gathering and said things he would not have said sober. He reviewed these the next day at length.
**Age 33 (719 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
**Age 34 (720 TR)** — It had not started as enmity. By the end, it was nothing else. Vala Alendyl became an enemy

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (703 TR)** — She pulled a door that pushed and then pushed it and it still did not open and someone eventually told her it was bolted.
> **Age 19 (704 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
> **Age 20 (705 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (706 TR)** — She watched the web of favours and enmities carefully, learning where to place her feet.
> **Age 22 (707 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 23 (708 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting.
> **Age 24 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 25 (710 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.
> **Age 26 (711 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 27 (712 TR)** — Patronage came with influence and obligations. She accepted both and managed the latter with care.
> **Age 28 (713 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 29 (714 TR)** — Matakea. The full title. She had spent seven years as Ashesa working toward this and the ceremony itself was over in an afternoon.
> **Age 30 (715 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 31 (716 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
> **Age 32 (717 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
> **Age 33 (718 TR)** — Music was part of a proper upbringing. She took the lessons seriously.
> **Age 34 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 35 (720 TR)** — She saw something that had turned violent and was not her affair. She noted it and walked on. She checked behind her twice.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (702 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
> **Age 19 (703 TR)** — The other apprentice got the better tasks, the master's patience, the benefit of the doubt. He got the corrections and the night work. He learned both crafts — the craft itself, and the craft of being the one who is not the favourite.
> **Age 20 (704 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
> **Age 21 (705 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
> **Age 22 (706 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
> **Age 23 (707 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
> **Age 24 (708 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
> **Age 25 (709 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
> **Age 27 (711 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
> **Age 28 (712 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.
> **Age 29 (713 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Edric Paring (lia_kavair)
> **Age 30 (714 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
> **Age 31 (715 TR)** — Someone he had trusted turned his confidence against him — for coin, for advantage, or from spite he never understood. Betrayed by Edric Paring
> **Age 32 (716 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.
> **Age 33 (717 TR)** — He sweeps the workshop, prepares the materials, cleans the tools, sweeps the workshop again. This is approximately seventy percent of his current working life. The other thirty percent is the actual work, which he is only permitted to do under supervision and at the low end of the skill scale. He is learning to see the difference between the work and the work — between the surface activity and the underlying structure of the craft. He is not certain this perception is accurate. He is fairly certain that the sweeping is not going to stop anytime soon.
> **Age 34 (718 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
> **Age 35 (719 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
> **Age 36 (720 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Terath Parsen died
narrative: **Age 18 (702 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.

**Age 19 (703 TR)** — The other apprentice got the better tasks, the master's patience, the benefit of the doubt. He got the corrections and the night work. He learned both crafts — the craft itself, and the craft of being the one who is not the favourite.

**Age 20 (704 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (705 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
**Age 22 (706 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
**Age 23 (707 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.

**Age 24 (708 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
**Age 25 (709 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
**Age 27 (711 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
**Age 28 (712 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.
**Age 29 (713 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Edric Paring (lia_kavair)
**Age 30 (714 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
**Age 31 (715 TR)** — Someone he had trusted turned his confidence against him — for coin, for advantage, or from spite he never understood. Betrayed by Edric Paring
**Age 32 (716 TR)** — Determined to regain his strength, he threw himself into physical discipline.

> **Sunsign:** Hirin  |  **Piety:** 19  |  **Born:** 10 Agrazhar

> **Archetype: Alewife** — She is not a guild member and does not want to be. She brews for her household and neighbourhood, sells to the inn down the road, and keeps within the guild law that permits this as long as she does not sell to the public directly. The line is interpreted generously in her village because there is no inn for three miles. She has been brewing the same recipe for twenty years. She does not write it down.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (701 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
> **Age 19 (702 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.
> **Age 20 (703 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (704 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Gorin Daling (guilded_innkeeper, age 26)
> **Age 21 (704 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Elva Daling lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Elva Daling born (female)
> **Age 22 (705 TR)** — She had trained her staff for exactly this eventuality, which she had always suspected was not a matter of if. The evacuation was clean. The loss was significant but not catastrophic: the taproom survived, the brewery survived, only the storage wing and the outer kitchen were gutted. She was trading again within the month. She had not been able to save the guest book, which annoyed her more than the structural damage.
> **Age 22 (705 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
> **Age 23 (706 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.
> **Age 23 (706 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 15
> **Age 24 (707 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.
> **Age 24 (707 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
> **Age 25 (708 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
> **Age 26 (709 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
> **Age 26 (709 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
> **Age 27 (710 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
> **Age 27 (710 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
> **Age 28 (711 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.
> **Age 28 (711 TR)** — She bore a child. Edric Daling came into the world healthy. week 40 Edric Daling born (male)
> **Age 29 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 29 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 31 (714 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.
> **Age 32 (715 TR)** — She rode out with hawk or hound when she could. The forest suited her.
> **Age 32 (715 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
> **Age 34 (717 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
> **Age 34 (717 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
> **Age 35 (718 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
> **Age 36 (719 TR)** — She had paid dues in bad years before. This year was different: the new baby, the tools she had replaced, the roof. She asked the chapter for a loan and was told to present her case to the syndics, which she did, in writing, which she had heard was more effective. The syndics granted the deferral for six months. She paid it back in five.
> **Age 36 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 36 (719 TR)** — She lost Edric Daling — still a child, not yet grown. Edric Daling died (age 8)
> **Age 37 (720 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
> **Age 37 (720 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (700 TR)** — Another family's child came to live under his roof — to learn, to serve as a page, or simply as a pledge of alliance. He found himself responsible for someone else's son. Took in Elora Maramin as ward
> **Age 19 (701 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
> **Age 20 (702 TR)** — The accolade fell on his shoulder. He rose a knight.
> **Age 21 (703 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
> **Age 22 (704 TR)** — The establishment was well run and the Shenasene who presided had a gift for making a man feel that losing money was a cultivated pleasure. He came out ahead, which he suspected was not the usual outcome and which made him more cautious rather than less on subsequent visits.
> **Age 23 (705 TR)** — It had begun as professional acquaintance. Somewhere in the course of years it became something else — not sentimentally, but in the way that trust accumulates. Elora Maramin became a close friend
> **Age 24 (706 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way.
> **Age 25 (707 TR)** — Someone chose him as the person they could tell. He was not sure it was a compliment. Confided in Hrolf Ilendi
> **Age 26 (708 TR)** — He realised he had been missing something that had been there all along. He was not sure how long he had been missing it.
> **Age 27 (709 TR)** — He called an important person by the wrong name in front of everyone. He thought about it for months.
> **Age 28 (710 TR)** — Lineage mattered. He learned to read shields and know their histories.
> **Age 29 (711 TR)** — He was taken into a lord's household, a position of modest honour and real obligation.
> **Age 30 (712 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Norath Uramin (noble, age 29)
> **Age 31 (713 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
> **Age 31 (713 TR)** — His wife's family connections opened new doors.
> **Age 32 (714 TR)** — It had not started as enmity. By the end, it was nothing else. Hrolf Ilendi became an enemy
> **Age 32 (714 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Andar Uramin — survived. week 40 Andar Uramin born (female)
> **Age 32 (714 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Andar Uramin died (age 0)
> **Age 33 (715 TR)** — He was certain of something and wrong. He argued his position at length before the evidence became impossible to ignore.
> **Age 34 (716 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Bram Ulekar
> **Age 34 (716 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Mora Uramin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Mora Uramin born (female)
> **Age 35 (717 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
> **Age 36 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 36 (718 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
> **Age 37 (719 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
> **Age 37 (719 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 10
> **Age 38 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 38 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (699 TR)** — She watched someone who really knew what they were doing. She had thought she knew. She saw now that she had been adequate.
> **Age 19 (700 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
> **Age 20 (701 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 21 (702 TR)** — She said something that was not true when the truth would have served equally well. She was puzzled by herself.
> **Age 22 (703 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Harlan Wainworth (merchant, age 26)
> **Age 22 (703 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 35
> **Age 23 (704 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Ruald Deepsack (merchant)
> **Age 23 (704 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
> **Age 24 (705 TR)** — She accompanied a caravan — widow, factor, or simply bold — to distant markets.
> **Age 25 (706 TR)** — She let a coin decide. It came down one way. Her reaction told her which way she had hoped it would land.
> **Age 25 (706 TR)** — She lost the child early — before she had said much aloud about it. She said nothing after, either. week 6
> **Age 26 (707 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
> **Age 26 (707 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. She did not speak of it openly. week 14
> **Age 27 (708 TR)** — The Trail was Pagaelin territory in every practical sense regardless of what the maps said. She learned its rhythms — which stretches drew attention, which seasons were dangerous, which small gestures communicated respect without communicating weakness.
> **Age 27 (708 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Rarith Wainworth. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Rarith Wainworth born (female)
> **Age 28 (709 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
> **Age 28 (709 TR)** — She bore a child. Kela Wainworth came into the world healthy. week 40 Kela Wainworth born (female)
> **Age 29 (710 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
> **Age 30 (711 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
> **Age 31 (712 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 32 (713 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
> **Age 32 (713 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 8
> **Age 33 (714 TR)** — She dreamed something that she decided not to speak of. She has never spoken of it.
> **Age 33 (714 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
> **Age 34 (715 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.
> **Age 34 (715 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
> **Age 35 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 36 (717 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
> **Age 36 (717 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
> **Age 37 (718 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 38 (719 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.
> **Age 38 (719 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Kela Wainworth apprenticed (age 10)
> **Age 39 (720 TR)** — She woke to a sound she could not account for. She lay still and listened until it stopped. Nothing explained it in the morning.
> **Age 39 (720 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.

**Age 33 (714 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
**Age 34 (715 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.
**Age 34 (715 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
**Age 35 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 36 (717 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
**Age 36 (717 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
**Age 37 (718 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.

**Age 38 (719 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.
**Age 38 (719 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Kela Wainworth apprenticed (age 10)
**Age 39 (720 TR)** — She woke to a sound she could not account for. She lay still and listened until it stopped. Nothing explained it in the morning.
**Age 39 (720 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (698 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
> **Age 19 (699 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.
> **Age 20 (700 TR)** — He marched under the sword-banner with a Matakea at the column's head and the Tirannon sung at dawn. It was harder than secular service — the discipline was absolute and the expectation of conduct was higher than any lord he had served — and there were moments he understood, without being able to explain, what the church was for.
> **Age 21 (701 TR)** — Months in a single fort or gatehouse. Cold mornings, bad food, and the same faces. He endured it.
> **Age 22 (702 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
> **Age 23 (703 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
> **Age 24 (704 TR)** — His lord acknowledged the deed — a purse, better arms, a public word of praise. It was enough.
> **Age 25 (705 TR)** — He found a coin in the road unlike any he had seen before. The merchant he showed it to grew oddly quiet.
> **Age 26 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 28 (708 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
> **Age 29 (709 TR)** — The Peonian sister arrived before he had finished sending for her and left after dawn with the kind of professional calm that made everything that had happened in between feel almost ordinary. He does not know her name. He knows the child is alive.
> **Age 30 (710 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Andar Laning (warrior, age 25)
> **Age 30 (710 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
> **Age 31 (711 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go.
> **Age 31 (711 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
> **Age 32 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 32 (712 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
> **Age 33 (713 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Roric Warson
> **Age 33 (713 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Mora Walson — survived. week 40 Mora Walson born (female)
> **Age 34 (714 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
> **Age 34 (714 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Sirath Walson. week 40 Sirath Walson born (female)
> **Age 35 (715 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
> **Age 35 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 36 (716 TR)** — When the coin was offered, he found reasons to look the other way.
> **Age 36 (716 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 5
> **Age 37 (717 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A serious wound to the head or face. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in eyesight (−1).
> **Age 37 (717 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
> **Age 38 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 39 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 39 (719 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
> **Age 40 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
