> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (697 TR)** — A secret entrusted to her found its way to those who would pay for it.
> **Age 19 (698 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.
> **Age 20 (699 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
> **Age 21 (700 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.
> **Age 22 (701 TR)** — The morning was so misty that she could see only a few steps ahead. She walked slowly and arrived late and was glad of both.
> **Age 23 (702 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
> **Age 24 (703 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
> **Age 25 (704 TR)** — There was a specific moment — she can identify it — when she did something in front of enough witnesses that the question of whether she belonged there stopped being asked. Not permanently, not universally; the question is never permanently retired. But in that unit, in that company, among those particular men, there was a before and an after. She did not celebrate. She did the next thing, and the thing after that. She is aware that the bar was set differently for her than for the men around her and that clearing it was not the same as having it set fairly. She cleared it anyway.
> **Age 26 (705 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
> **Age 27 (706 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
> **Age 28 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 29 (708 TR)** — She performed an old ritual her grandmother had taught her. She felt better afterwards and did not explain it.
> **Age 30 (709 TR)** — When the coin was offered, she found reasons to look the other way.
> **Age 31 (710 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
> **Age 32 (711 TR)** — She sneezed at the worst possible moment. The ceremony continued. The people nearest her found it difficult to maintain appropriate expressions.
> **Age 33 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 34 (713 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
> **Age 35 (714 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
> **Age 36 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 37 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 38 (717 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
> **Age 39 (718 TR)** — She had thought herself a better judge of odds than she was. The coin was gone before she had fully reckoned what she'd risked.
> **Age 40 (719 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.
> **Age 41 (720 TR)** — Age made its presence felt. She adapted, but felt the difference. The illness passed without lasting harm.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (696 TR)** — A dog followed him home one evening and refused to leave. He let it stay.
> **Age 19 (697 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
> **Age 20 (698 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
> **Age 21 (699 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the abdomen that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
> **Age 22 (700 TR)** — An ordinary market day stayed with him for years. He could not say what had been notable about it.
> **Age 23 (701 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Caris Cooper (artisan, age 16)
> **Age 23 (701 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
> **Age 24 (702 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
> **Age 24 (702 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Helra Halman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Helra Halman born (female)
> **Age 24 (702 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Helra Halman died (age 0)
> **Age 25 (703 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
> **Age 25 (703 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
> **Age 26 (704 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
> **Age 26 (704 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Xarin Halman born (male)
> **Age 26 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 27 (705 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
> **Age 28 (706 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
> **Age 28 (706 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Hadric Halman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Hadric Halman born (male)
> **Age 29 (707 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
> **Age 29 (707 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
> **Age 30 (708 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
> **Age 30 (708 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Talar Halman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Talar Halman born (male)
> **Age 31 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 32 (710 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
> **Age 32 (710 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
> **Age 33 (711 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
> **Age 34 (712 TR)** — He was asked to take sides in a dispute between two neighbours. He tried to remain neutral. Both sides felt he had betrayed them.
> **Age 34 (712 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 37
> **Age 35 (713 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
> **Age 36 (714 TR)** — He ate the best meal of his life at an unremarkable place, served by someone who did not seem aware of the achievement.
> **Age 37 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 37 (715 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
> **Age 38 (716 TR)** — He finally tried something he had refused for years and found it was fine. This was annoying.
> **Age 39 (717 TR)** — He watched young men doing exactly what he had done at their age and felt something he could not entirely name.
> **Age 39 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 40 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 41 (719 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Varen Koring died
> **Age 41 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 42 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 42 (720 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.

**Age 26 (704 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Xarin Halman born (male)
**Age 26 (704 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 27 (705 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
**Age 28 (706 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 28 (706 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Hadric Halman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Hadric Halman born (male)

**Age 29 (707 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 29 (707 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 30 (708 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 30 (708 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Talar Halman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Talar Halman born (male)
**Age 31 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 32 (710 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
**Age 32 (710 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.

**Age 33 (711 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
**Age 34 (712 TR)** — He was asked to take sides in a dispute between two neighbours. He tried to remain neutral. Both sides felt he had betrayed them.
**Age 34 (712 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 37

**Age 35 (713 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
**Age 36 (714 TR)** — He ate the best meal of his life at an unremarkable place, served by someone who did not seem aware of the achievement.
**Age 37 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 37 (715 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 38 (716 TR)** — He finally tried something he had refused for years and found it was fine. This was annoying.
**Age 39 (717 TR)** — He watched young men doing exactly what he had done at their age and felt something he could not entirely name.
**Age 39 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 40 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (719 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Varen Koring died
**Age 41 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (720 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (695 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
> **Age 19 (696 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
> **Age 20 (697 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (698 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
> **Age 22 (699 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
> **Age 23 (700 TR)** — The chemical burned a stripe across the back of her wrist when the vessel cracked unexpectedly. She poured water over it immediately, which was the right response, and the burn is less bad than it might have been. She knows now what the material does under certain conditions, and she knows it in a way that reading about it did not produce. The wrist heals. The knowledge stays.
> **Age 24 (701 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Elric Dyer (artisan, age 23)
> **Age 24 (701 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
> **Age 25 (702 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
> **Age 25 (702 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
> **Age 26 (703 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Jorin Harper
> **Age 26 (703 TR)** — She lost the child early — before she had said much aloud about it. She said nothing after, either. week 8
> **Age 27 (704 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
> **Age 27 (704 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
> **Age 28 (705 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands.
> **Age 28 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 29 (706 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.
> **Age 29 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 30 (707 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
> **Age 31 (708 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them.
> **Age 31 (708 TR)** — She bore a child. Rarith Dyer came into the world healthy. week 40 Rarith Dyer born (female)
> **Age 31 (708 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Rarith Dyer died (age 0)
> **Age 32 (709 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
> **Age 32 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 33 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 33 (710 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
> **Age 34 (711 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Jorin Harper became a close friend
> **Age 34 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 35 (712 TR)** — She took her loom to her mother's house and worked from there for a year. The guild does not certify home-weavers; this is an area where the guild's coverage is incomplete by design, because the women who weave at home are not the women the guild is designed to serve. She is not sure whether this is convenient or an insult. She sold everything she produced and used the money to buy better materials. Then she went back to the guild workshop.
> **Age 35 (712 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 11
> **Age 36 (713 TR)** — The piece was good enough that the mistress put it in the window. This is a specific honour — pieces in the window are examples of what the workshop produces, and only the journeymen's work goes there as a rule. The mistress did not comment on it being apprentice work. She did not need to. The placement said what needed to be said. She has spent considerable time looking at the piece from the street, which she knows is a character flaw, and which she intends to stop once she has made a second piece of equal quality.
> **Age 36 (713 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 37
> **Age 37 (714 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
> **Age 38 (715 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
> **Age 38 (715 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Selda Dyer lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Selda Dyer born (female)
> **Age 39 (716 TR)** — She grieved thoroughly and privately. When she came through it she was quieter than she had been, and harder to read.
> **Age 39 (716 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
> **Age 40 (717 TR)** — A tune she caught half of at a festival lodged in her head for the rest of the season.
> **Age 40 (717 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
> **Age 41 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 41 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 42 (719 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Teda Strongpurse (merchant)
> **Age 42 (719 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
> **Age 43 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 43 (720 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.

**Age 39 (716 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
**Age 40 (717 TR)** — A tune she caught half of at a festival lodged in her head for the rest of the season.

**Age 40 (717 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
**Age 41 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 42 (719 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Teda Strongpurse (merchant)
**Age 42 (719 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 43 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (720 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (694 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
> **Age 19 (695 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Zara Vorsen (unguilded, age 16)
> **Age 20 (696 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
> **Age 20 (696 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
> **Age 21 (697 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
> **Age 21 (697 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Bram Rorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Bram Rorman born (male)
> **Age 22 (698 TR)** — He dreamed the same dream three nights running. It was vivid and made no sense. He did not speak of it.
> **Age 22 (698 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Varath Rorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Varath Rorman born (female)
> **Age 23 (699 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
> **Age 23 (699 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
> **Age 24 (700 TR)** — He did something he had been told as a child to ward off bad luck. He was aware of how it looked. He did it anyway.
> **Age 24 (700 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 26
> **Age 25 (701 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
> **Age 25 (701 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Rira Rorman. week 40 Rira Rorman born (female)
> **Age 26 (702 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
> **Age 26 (702 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
> **Age 27 (703 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
> **Age 27 (703 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Lothar Rorman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Lothar Rorman born (male)
> **Age 28 (704 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
> **Age 29 (705 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
> **Age 29 (705 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
> **Age 30 (706 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
> **Age 30 (706 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
> **Age 31 (707 TR)** — He found his first grey hair. He pulled it out, then felt foolish, then stopped caring.
> **Age 31 (707 TR)** — Zara Vorsen died. They had been married 12 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Zara Vorsen died (approx. age 28)
> **Age 32 (708 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
> **Age 32 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 33 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 33 (709 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
> **Age 34 (710 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
> **Age 34 (710 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
> **Age 34 (710 TR)** — Varath Rorman was nearly grown when she died. Varath Rorman died (age 12)
> **Age 35 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 35 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 36 (712 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it.
> **Age 37 (713 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
> **Age 37 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 38 (714 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
> **Age 38 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 39 (715 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Erek Halman (unguilded)
> **Age 40 (716 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
> **Age 41 (717 TR)** — He passed a milestone stone he had passed a hundred times and stopped for no reason he could explain. He stood there a while.
> **Age 41 (717 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
> **Age 41 (717 TR)** — His daughter Rira Rorman married Dorin Garkin. Rira Rorman married Dorin Garkin (peasant, age 18)
> **Age 42 (718 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
> **Age 42 (718 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Lothar Rorman fostered (age 15)
> **Age 43 (719 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
> **Age 43 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 43 (719 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Rira Rorman's child born (male, Rira Rorman age 18)
> **Age 44 (720 TR)** — He came home to the door off its hinges and the strongbox open. They had taken the silver plate, his wife's jewellery, and the tools he had been saving for three years. The worst of it was not the loss — it was the knowledge that someone had been through everything, had touched everything, had made themselves at home in his absence.
> **Age 44 (720 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
> **Age 44 (720 TR)** — His son Lothar Rorman married Calla Valson. Lothar Rorman married Calla Valson (unguilded, age 16)

# Zara

> [!gm] GM Notes

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (693 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
> **Age 19 (694 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
> **Age 20 (695 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (696 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
> **Age 22 (697 TR)** — She said something that was not true when the truth would have served equally well. She was puzzled by herself.
> **Age 23 (698 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
> **Age 24 (699 TR)** — She read what texts she could find, and the night sky taught her the rest.
> **Age 25 (700 TR)** — Loss drove her inward; she spent months in solitary reflection.
> **Age 26 (701 TR)** — She wept at something small and ordinary. She was not embarrassed. She let it pass.
> **Age 27 (702 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.
> **Age 28 (703 TR)** — Matakea. The full title. She had spent seven years as Ashesa working toward this and the ceremony itself was over in an afternoon.
> **Age 29 (704 TR)** — A military posting for a woman of the cloth was unusual. She received it as a mark of trust and declined to read it any other way.
> **Age 30 (705 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
> **Age 31 (706 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
> **Age 32 (707 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
> **Age 33 (708 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
> **Age 34 (709 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
> **Age 35 (710 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected.
> **Age 36 (711 TR)** — A named posting — chaplain to a Lady of Paladins chapter. The knights watched her carefully for the first month to see how she would take the work.
> **Age 37 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 38 (713 TR)** — She walked to the shrine. The journey changed something — not in the way she expected, which was perhaps what it was supposed to do.
> **Age 39 (714 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 40 (715 TR)** — She began writing — not because anyone asked, but because it needed doing.
> **Age 41 (716 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting.
> **Age 42 (717 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark.
> **Age 43 (718 TR)** — She caught sight of something that was already past by the time she looked. She mentioned it to no one.
> **Age 44 (719 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 45 (720 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (692 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
> **Age 19 (693 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
> **Age 20 (694 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
> **Age 21 (695 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 22 (696 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
> **Age 23 (697 TR)** — A wife came into his household. He was not certain what he had expected, but he had not expected how much adjustment it required. Married Helra Lorman (lia_kavair, age 17)
> **Age 23 (697 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Edric Idkin born (male)
> **Age 24 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 24 (698 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
> **Age 25 (699 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
> **Age 25 (699 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
> **Age 26 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 26 (700 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
> **Age 27 (701 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.
> **Age 27 (701 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
> **Age 28 (702 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard.
> **Age 28 (702 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
> **Age 29 (703 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working.
> **Age 29 (703 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
> **Age 30 (704 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
> **Age 30 (704 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
> **Age 31 (705 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
> **Age 31 (705 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Tovan Idkin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Tovan Idkin born (male)
> **Age 32 (706 TR)** — The fence operates from a chandlery in the river quarter and has been in business for twenty years, which in his line of work means either exceptional competence or exceptional luck, and he is not a lucky-looking man. **Age 20 (694 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
**Age 21 (695 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 22 (696 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.

He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard.
**Age 28 (702 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 19 (692 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Noral Cooper (artisan, age 25)
> **Age 19 (692 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
> **Age 20 (693 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 20 (693 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 33
> **Age 21 (694 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
> **Age 23 (696 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.
> **Age 23 (696 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
> **Age 24 (697 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
> **Age 24 (697 TR)** — She bore a child. Deric Cooper came into the world healthy. week 40 Deric Cooper born (male)
> **Age 25 (698 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 25 (698 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Rora Cooper lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Rora Cooper born (female)
> **Age 26 (699 TR)** — The harvest failed. She stretched what remained as far as it would go.
> **Age 26 (699 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
> **Age 27 (700 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
> **Age 27 (700 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
> **Age 28 (701 TR)** — The child lived. She could see from the beginning that they would need more — more care, more patience, more of everything she had. She did not hesitate. week 40 — simple Tovan Cooper born (male, simple-minded)
> **Age 28 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 29 (702 TR)** — The creditors came. What she had built was gone.
> **Age 29 (702 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
> **Age 30 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 31 (704 TR)** — She left for the town — widow, runaway, or simply desperate. The village was behind her.
> **Age 31 (704 TR)** — She caught herself watching the child too long, calculating futures. She stopped herself and went back to work. The watching started again an hour later.
> **Age 32 (705 TR)** — She was the first person to notice that spring was coming. She did not announce it. She kept it to herself for a few days.
> **Age 32 (705 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Seric Cooper lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Seric Cooper born (male)
> **Age 32 (705 TR)** — She lost Tovan Cooper — the simple-minded child she had given so much to. Tovan Cooper died (age 4)
> **Age 33 (706 TR)** — She saw a man killed — not in war, but in the world she moved through every day. The image did not leave her easily.
> **Age 33 (706 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Theric Cooper lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Theric Cooper born (male)
> **Age 33 (706 TR)** — She lost Rora Cooper — still a child, not yet grown. Rora Cooper died (age 8)
> **Age 34 (707 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.
> **Age 34 (707 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Alda Cooper. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Alda Cooper born (female)
> **Age 35 (708 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
> **Age 36 (709 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
> **Age 37 (710 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Zoric Aldane (noble)
> **Age 37 (710 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Cador Cooper — lived. week 40 Cador Cooper born (male)
> **Age 37 (710 TR)** — She lost Alda Cooper — still a child, not yet grown. Alda Cooper died (age 3)
> **Age 38 (711 TR)** — The lord's deer were fat and the larder thin; she took the risk.
> **Age 38 (711 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Antor Cooper. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Antor Cooper born (male)
> **Age 39 (712 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the forearm or hand that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
> **Age 39 (712 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
> **Age 40 (713 TR)** — Age made its presence felt. She adapted, but felt the difference. The illness passed without lasting harm.
> **Age 40 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 41 (714 TR)** — Her mother died in winter. The heriot was due before spring. The lord's steward arrived in February and assessed the estate at a figure that would have required selling the cow, which would have made the spring planting impossible. She disputed the assessment formally at the village meeting, citing the customary rate for holdings of the size and type. The steward revised the assessment. She paid the heriot and kept the cow. She planted in April. The harvest was adequate.
> **Age 41 (714 TR)** — Deric Cooper had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her. Deric Cooper died (age 17)
> **Age 42 (715 TR)** — A fox paused on the path and looked at her with calm, appraising eyes before trotting away.
> **Age 42 (715 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
> **Age 43 (716 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — she took what was not hers.
> **Age 43 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 43 (716 TR)** — She lost Antor Cooper — still a child, not yet grown. Antor Cooper died (age 5)
> **Age 44 (717 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
> **Age 44 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 45 (718 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Hadric Soring
> **Age 45 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 46 (719 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 46 (719 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
> **Age 47 (720 TR)** — The last coin went on the fourth day of the cold. She had plans — there are always plans — but plans require a material floor and she had passed below it. She knew the Peonian house was on the south side of the market quarter. She went there. A sister opened the door and did not ask questions about how she had arrived here. She received a meal, a bed, a day's grace. She has been operating at the margin between those two realities ever since: the bed and what comes before the bed.
> **Age 47 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

narrative: **Age 19 (692 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Noral Cooper (artisan, age 25)
**Age 19 (692 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.

**Age 20 (693 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
**Age 20 (693 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 33

**Age 21 (694 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.

**Age 23 (696 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.
**Age 23 (696 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.

**Age 24 (697 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
**Age 24 (697 TR)** — She bore a child. Deric Cooper came into the world healthy. week 40 Deric Cooper born (male)
**Age 25 (698 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 25 (698 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Rora Cooper lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Rora Cooper born (female)
**Age 26 (699 TR)** — The harvest failed. She stretched what remained as far as it would go.
**Age 26 (699 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.

**Age 27 (700 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
**Age 27 (700 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.

**Age 28 (701 TR)** — The child lived. She could see from the beginning that they would need more — more care, more patience, more of everything she had. She did not hesitate. week 40 — simple Tovan Cooper born (male, simple-minded)
**Age 28 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 29 (702 TR)** — The creditors came. What she had built was gone.
**Age 29 (702 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
**Age 30 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 31 (704 TR)** — She left for the town — widow, runaway, or simply desperate. The village was behind her.
**Age 31 (704 TR)** — She caught herself watching the child too long, calculating futures. She stopped herself and went back to work. The watching started again an hour later.
**Age 32 (705 TR)** — She was the first person to notice that spring was coming. She did not announce it. She kept it to herself for a few days.
**Age 32 (705 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Seric Cooper lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Seric Cooper born (male)
**Age 32 (705 TR)** — She lost Tovan Cooper — the simple-minded child she had given so much to. Tovan Cooper died (age 4)

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (690 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.
> **Age 19 (691 TR)** — He returned a wave that had not been directed at him. He kept walking. The person behind him waved back.
> **Age 20 (692 TR)** — The accolade fell on his shoulder. He rose a knight.
> **Age 21 (693 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Dara Falendi (noble, age 20)
> **Age 21 (693 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Orla Falendi born (female)
> **Age 22 (694 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
> **Age 23 (695 TR)** — His eldest brother was dead. But there were others ahead of him still. The land was not his.
> **Age 23 (695 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Melda Falendi — survived. week 40 Melda Falendi born (female)
> **Age 24 (696 TR)** — He rode at the lists and acquitted himself respectably.
> **Age 24 (696 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
> **Age 25 (697 TR)** — He found someone who knew more than he did and paid in time and deference. Studied under Brand
> **Age 25 (697 TR)** — The child came at term and did not live. He had thought the danger past. week 38
> **Age 26 (698 TR)** — He was certain of something and wrong. He argued his position at length before the evidence became impossible to ignore.
> **Age 27 (699 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
> **Age 27 (699 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 32
> **Age 28 (700 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
> **Age 28 (700 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
> **Age 29 (701 TR)** — Circumstance put him in front of an audience. He found he could hold them.
> **Age 29 (701 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
> **Age 29 (701 TR)** — Orla Falendi died young — a child still. Orla Falendi died (age 8)
> **Age 30 (702 TR)** — He stopped what he was doing to watch a sunrise. He was late for something. He did not care.
> **Age 30 (702 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
> **Age 31 (703 TR)** — A bird built its nest in the eaves above his door. He left it alone all season.
> **Age 31 (703 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
> **Age 32 (704 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. > **Age 33 (705 TR)** — The sleeve shifted, or the light caught it wrong. He saw the moment the other man understood what he was looking at. The conversation changed.
> **Age 33 (705 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
> **Age 34 (706 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
> **Age 34 (706 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
> **Age 35 (707 TR)** — He made a vow at the chapel before the campaign: if he returned, he would give a year's service in Larani's name. He returned. He spent considerable time afterward determining what a year's service meant and whether he was fulfilling it adequately.
> **Age 36 (708 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
> **Age 36 (708 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Tara Falendi came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Tara Falendi born (female)
> **Age 37 (709 TR)** — He said something that needed saying when everyone else was quiet. He would rather someone else had said it.
> **Age 37 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 38 (710 TR)** — He gave advice that was followed. He spent years wondering if it was good advice.
> **Age 39 (711 TR)** — He told a small lie that had no purpose. The truth would have been fine. He did not know why he had said it.
> **Age 39 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

> **Age 40 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (689 TR)** — She accompanied a caravan — widow, factor, or simply bold — to distant markets.
> **Age 19 (690 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Orin Longhaul (merchant, age 16)
> **Age 19 (690 TR)** — She and her husband argued about something entirely trivial. Neither of them could remember exactly what it was. The silence lasted four days.
> **Age 20 (691 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
> **Age 20 (691 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
> **Age 21 (692 TR)** — She was up before anyone and watched the sun come up from somewhere good. She did not tell anyone about it.
> **Age 21 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 22 (693 TR)** — She heard something that would have been wonderful at the time but reached her so late that it was only quietly pleasant.
> **Age 23 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 23 (694 TR)** — The match her family arranged proved more fortunate than she had expected.
> **Age 24 (695 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Arwen Goldcloak (merchant)
> **Age 25 (696 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 25 (696 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 37
> **Age 26 (697 TR)** — She waited in line. She was next. The thing she had been waiting for was gone.
> **Age 26 (697 TR)** — She bore a child. Wela Longhaul came into the world healthy. week 40 Wela Longhaul born (female)
> **Age 27 (698 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
> **Age 28 (699 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.
> **Age 28 (699 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 15
> **Age 29 (700 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
> **Age 29 (700 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
> **Age 30 (701 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
> **Age 30 (701 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 8
> **Age 31 (702 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
> **Age 32 (703 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Arwen Goldcloak became a close friend
> **Age 32 (703 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
> **Age 33 (704 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
> **Age 34 (705 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
> **Age 34 (705 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.
> **Age 35 (706 TR)** — A task she had estimated at two hours became an entire day. She adjusted.
> **Age 36 (707 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Bram Ironworth
> **Age 36 (707 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
> **Age 37 (708 TR)** — She called someone by their dead sibling's name. The silence that followed lasted a long time.
> **Age 38 (709 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
> **Age 38 (709 TR)** — Orin Longhaul died after 19 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Orin Longhaul died (approx. age 35)
> **Age 39 (710 TR)** — She smelled something that stopped her dead in the street. It brought back a whole season she had thought she had forgotten.
> **Age 39 (710 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
> **Age 40 (711 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back.
> **Age 40 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 41 (712 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
> **Age 42 (713 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.
> **Age 43 (714 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with.
> **Age 44 (715 TR)** — She ran her hand over something and the feeling of it brought back a place and a time she had not thought of in years.
> **Age 45 (716 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 46 (717 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Neda Coinsworth
> **Age 47 (718 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
> **Age 47 (718 TR)** — Wela Longhaul left to make her own way. Wela Longhaul left home (age 21)
> **Age 48 (719 TR)** — She finished something and found she was satisfied with it. That was rarer than it should have been.
> **Age 48 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 49 (720 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
