**Age 25 (696 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 37
**Age 26 (697 TR)** — She waited in line. She was next. The thing she had been waiting for was gone.
**Age 26 (697 TR)** — She bore a child. Wela Longhaul came into the world healthy. week 40 Wela Longhaul born (female)
**Age 27 (698 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 28 (699 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.
**Age 28 (699 TR)** — Another loss before it had become a child in any way others would recognise. She grieved it nonetheless. week 15
**Age 29 (700 TR)** — A finger was pointed at her — whether justly or not, her name was tainted.
**Age 29 (700 TR)** — She led her family confidently in entirely the wrong direction. They did not find this out until they were well off course. They were eventually fine.
**Age 30 (701 TR)** — She stood in the ruins and decided what came next.
**Age 30 (701 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 8

**Age 31 (702 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.

**Age 32 (703 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Arwen Goldcloak became a close friend
**Age 32 (703 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
**Age 33 (704 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.

**Age 34 (705 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
**Age 34 (705 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.
**Age 35 (706 TR)** — A task she had estimated at two hours became an entire day. She adjusted.
**Age 36 (707 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Bram Ironworth
**Age 36 (707 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
**Age 37 (708 TR)** — She called someone by their dead sibling's name. The silence that followed lasted a long time.

**Age 38 (709 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 38 (709 TR)** — Orin Longhaul died after 19 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Orin Longhaul died (approx. age 35)
**Age 39 (710 TR)** — She smelled something that stopped her dead in the street. It brought back a whole season she had thought she had forgotten.
**Age 39 (710 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
**Age 40 (711 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back.
**Age 40 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (712 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
**Age 42 (713 TR)** — The court found her guilty. What followed was swift and public.

**Age 43 (714 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with.
**Age 44 (715 TR)** — She ran her hand over something and the feeling of it brought back a place and a time she had not thought of in years.
**Age 45 (716 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 46 (717 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Neda Coinsworth
**Age 47 (718 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
**Age 47 (718 TR)** — Wela Longhaul left to make her own way. Wela Longhaul left home (age 21)
**Age 48 (719 TR)** — She finished something and found she was satisfied with it. That was rarer than it should have been.
**Age 48 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (720 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (688 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
> **Age 19 (689 TR)** — His lord no longer needed him, or no longer wanted him. He was paid off and told to go.
> **Age 20 (690 TR)** — The knife came out of the dark on the Kolisa bridge. He was lucky — the cobblestones were wet and he slipped at the wrong moment, which turned out to be the right moment. He did not see the face. He lay in the mud listening to footsteps retreating and understood that someone had spent money to end his life and received inadequate value.
> **Age 22 (692 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
> **Age 23 (693 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
> **Age 24 (694 TR)** — He watched a true expert do something he had done himself for years. He understood the difference immediately.
> **Age 25 (695 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
> **Age 26 (696 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
> **Age 27 (697 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
> **Age 28 (698 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Aleth Warman (peasant, age 28)
> **Age 29 (699 TR)** — Campaign rations or lean winters had made him a competent cook.
> **Age 29 (699 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
> **Age 30 (700 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting.
> **Age 30 (700 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
> **Age 31 (701 TR)** — The first real cold came and he realised summer was over. He stood in it for a moment before going inside.
> **Age 32 (702 TR)** — Drink, pride, or an old grudge turned into something harder. He gave as good as he got.
> **Age 32 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 33 (703 TR)** — He had about forty words of Pagaelin and a captive who knew what all forty of them were. The Pagaelin watched him assemble his questions with an expression that was not quite contempt — closer to professional assessment. He got nothing useful and the captive escaped two days later, which he found he could not entirely resent.
> **Age 33 (703 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
> **Age 34 (704 TR)** — A lord or town sergeant offered him steady pay and a bunk. He stopped moving.
> **Age 34 (704 TR)** — Aleth Warman died. They had been married 6 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Aleth Warman died (approx. age 34)
> **Age 35 (705 TR)** — He heard something outside in the night, got up to investigate, and found nothing. He went back to bed and lay awake for an hour.
> **Age 36 (706 TR)** — Not fluency — nothing close to it. Enough to tell from tone whether the next thing would be a toll demand or a spear throw. Enough to count. Enough to say no and mean it, or yes and be understood. The rest he learned to read from posture.
> **Age 37 (707 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Yoric Lalekar (noble)
> **Age 37 (707 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
> **Age 38 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 39 (709 TR)** — The letter arrived with no signature. It described, in precise and accurate detail, something he had done three years ago that he had believed no one knew. The terms were not unreasonable. That was almost the worst part — whoever had sent it understood exactly what the information was worth and had priced it accordingly.
> **Age 40 (710 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Voran Halman
> **Age 41 (711 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A serious wound to the chest or torso. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).
> **Age 42 (712 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
> **Age 43 (713 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
> **Age 44 (714 TR)** — His lord had given an order he could not follow. Not would not — could not, in the sense that following it would have made him someone he did not want to be and could not have looked at in still water. He refused, formally, with witnesses. He was not punished. That surprised him more than the order had.
> **Age 45 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 46 (716 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Lothar Maling died
> **Age 47 (717 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
> **Age 48 (718 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
> **Age 49 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 50 (720 TR)** — Steel found him. The wound healed, after a fashion.

>

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (687 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
> **Age 19 (688 TR)** — She entered her husband's household. The first months were an exercise in understanding the rules of a different world. Married Uther Tarsen (soldier, age 19)
> **Age 19 (688 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
> **Age 20 (689 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 21 (690 TR)** — The sergeant put her on the worst assignment in the rotation — the extended perimeter watch, the night gate, the escort duty nobody wanted. She took every assignment without comment. She did every assignment better than the man who had held it before. This took approximately eight months to become visible to the chain of command, which is the standard timeline for this particular kind of proof. At the end of it she was getting normal assignments. She noted that none of the men had been required to run this specific trial. She also noted that having run it had made her better at the job than most of the men who had not.
> **Age 21 (690 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Edris Tarsen. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Edris Tarsen born (female)
> **Age 22 (691 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Meric Finecoin (merchant)
> **Age 22 (691 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
> **Age 23 (692 TR)** — The order was to burn the village. The people were Morgathian sympathisers, the officer said, which may even have been true. She refused in front of the column and said Larani's name when she did it. There was a silence. The officer did not repeat the order. She has never understood why.
> **Age 23 (692 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Neda Tarsen lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Neda Tarsen born (female)
> **Age 24 (693 TR)** — The same dream visited her three nights. She woke each time unsettled and unable to say why.
> **Age 24 (693 TR)** — She watched her parent simply get on with things during a hard stretch. She learned something about it that stayed with her.
> **Age 25 (694 TR)** — She was assigned to the Oselmarch border. The garrison was small, the range was wide, and the Pagaelin were an omnipresent fact of life rather than an abstract threat. She learned to read the heath the way the long-service soldiers did — not as landscape but as communication.
> **Age 25 (694 TR)** — Her child attempted a household task without help or permission. They were very proud. She let them be proud while she quietly fixed it.
> **Age 26 (695 TR)** — The child was lost before it had become real to anyone else. She marked it privately. week 6
> **Age 27 (696 TR)** — She is a widow visiting her sister's family, which is why she is on this road, which is why she has stopped at this inn, which is why she is asking about the road ahead to the next village. None of this is true. All of it holds. She has learned that the most sustainable cover is one that requires the minimum number of lies — the one that lets the truth do as much of the work as possible.
> **Age 27 (696 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Rorath Tarsen — lived. week 40 Rorath Tarsen born (female)
> **Age 28 (697 TR)** — Determined to regain her strength, she threw herself into physical discipline.
> **Age 28 (697 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Arith Tarsen lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Arith Tarsen born (female)
> **Age 29 (698 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Terath Tarsen lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Terath Tarsen born (female)
> **Age 30 (699 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
> **Age 30 (699 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Dorin Tarsen lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Dorin Tarsen born (male)
> **Age 31 (700 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.
> **Age 32 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 32 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 32 (701 TR)** — She lost Arith Tarsen — still a child, not yet grown. Arith Tarsen died (age 4)
> **Age 32 (701 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Dorin Tarsen died (age 2)
> **Age 33 (702 TR)** — She was not devout. She went to the Matakea the night before the assault because several people she respected had done the same, and because the mathematics of the next day were not reassuring. She did not find God in the conversation. She found something else — a clarity about what she was willing to die for, which turned out to be enough.
> **Age 33 (702 TR)** — She lost the child early — before she had said much aloud about it. She said nothing after, either. week 4
> **Age 34 (703 TR)** — She made a small thing out of available scraps — braided, sewn, shaped. She kept it without being able to say why.
> **Age 34 (703 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Ulric Tarsen lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Ulric Tarsen born (male)
> **Age 34 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 35 (704 TR)** — The vow was made in private, to the sword-altar, in language she had not planned and could not quite reproduce afterward. She survived. She has been paying the debt ever since in ways she has not always been able to name — small acts, a certain quality of attention, a refusal of one particular easy choice, repeated.
> **Age 35 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 36 (705 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
> **Age 36 (705 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
> **Age 36 (705 TR)** — She lost Rorath Tarsen — still a child, not yet grown. Rorath Tarsen died (age 9)
> **Age 37 (706 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
> **Age 37 (706 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
> **Age 37 (706 TR)** — Her daughter Edris Tarsen married Loran Dyer. Edris Tarsen married Loran Dyer (artisan, age 16)
> **Age 37 (706 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Edris Tarsen's child born (male, Edris Tarsen age 16)
> **Age 38 (707 TR)** — She stopped at an old waymarker she knew well. She had never stopped there before. She could not say what made her stop.
> **Age 38 (707 TR)** — Uther Tarsen died after 19 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Uther Tarsen died (approx. age 38)
> **Age 39 (708 TR)** — She stood somewhere and saw something beautiful. She kept the image of it longer than most things.
> **Age 39 (708 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
> **Age 40 (709 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
> **Age 40 (709 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
> **Age 41 (710 TR)** — The cover held but it was close. A merchant's wife asked her about a village she claimed to be from and knew it in a level of detail she didn't — specific names, specific streets. She deflected smoothly enough but the deflection was noticed; she saw it noticed. She completed the task and returned and asked her officer directly whether she needed to change the cover identity. He said not yet. She is keeping very careful track of what 'not yet' means in practice.
> **Age 41 (710 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Terath Tarsen hired out (age 12)
> **Age 41 (710 TR)** — Her daughter Neda Tarsen married Orlan Warsen. Neda Tarsen married Orlan Warsen (soldier, age 22)
> **Age 41 (710 TR)** — Neda Tarsen left to make her own way. Neda Tarsen left home (age 18)
> **Age 42 (711 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.
> **Age 42 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 43 (712 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
> **Age 43 (712 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
> **Age 44 (713 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
> **Age 44 (713 TR)** — Edris Tarsen left to make her own way. Edris Tarsen left home (age 23)
> **Age 45 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 45 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 46 (715 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 47 (716 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
> **Age 48 (717 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
> **Age 48 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 49 (718 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Disa Valkin died
> **Age 49 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 50 (719 TR)** — She picked it up the way all border people do — imperfectly, practically, with no interest in the grammar. Numbers, directions, threat indicators, the specific word for leave-now-or-die. She had heard it used correctly once and that was enough.
> **Age 51 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 51 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 51 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 51 (720 TR)** — Terath Tarsen died before she had expected it. The grief of outliving a child did not diminish with their age. Terath Tarsen died (age 22)

She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
**Age 40 (709 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.

**Age 41 (710 TR)** — The cover held but it was close. A merchant's wife asked her about a village she claimed to be from and knew it in a level of detail she didn't — specific names, specific streets. She deflected smoothly enough but the deflection was noticed; she saw it noticed. She completed the task and returned and asked her officer directly whether she needed to change the cover identity. He said not yet. She is keeping very careful track of what 'not yet' means in practice.
**Age 41 (710 TR)** — She sent one of her children out to be apprenticed or fostered. It was the right provision. The house was quieter for it. Terath Tarsen hired out (age 12)
**Age 41 (710 TR)** — Her daughter Neda Tarsen married Orlan Warsen. Neda Tarsen married Orlan Warsen (soldier, age 22)
**Age 41 (710 TR)** — Neda Tarsen left to make her own way. Neda Tarsen left home (age 18)
**Age 42 (711 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.
**Age 42 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 43 (712 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
**Age 43 (712 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.

**Age 44 (713 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 44 (713 TR)** — Edris Tarsen left to make her own way. Edris Tarsen left home (age 23)
**Age 45 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 45 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (715 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 47 (716 TR)** — The loss required time she had not expected to spend on it. She gave the time. There was no other option.
**Age 48 (717 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 48 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (718 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Disa Valkin died
**Age 49 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 50 (719 TR)** — She picked it up the way all border people do — imperfectly, practically, with no interest in the grammar. Numbers, directions, threat indicators, the specific word for leave-now-or-die. She had heard it used correctly once and that was enough.
**Age 51 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 51 (720 TR)** — Terath Tarsen died before she had expected it. The grief of outliving a child did not diminish with their age. Terath Tarsen died (age 22)

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (686 TR)** — He left in November, which is the worst time to travel and the best time to leave unnoticed — the roads are empty and the lord's reeve does not expect movement. He reached the chartered town in four days and presented himself to the town officer and declared his intent to remain. The year and a day is not a comfortable year. It is a year of knowing that anyone from the home village who recognises you and reports to your lord can end it. He did the work available to him, he stayed within the town boundaries, he did not draw attention. At the end of the year and a day the town officer acknowledged him a freeman. He has never returned to the village. He sends money when he can.
> **Age 19 (687 TR)** — The lord's steward reclassified him as a serf when the estate changed hands. He disputed it. The dispute went to the hundred moot, which is one of the few places where a man can contest whether he is free, because the question of whether he is free cannot be decided by the lord's court — the lord's court does not have jurisdiction over a man who may not be in the lord's jurisdiction. He obtained the writ himself, which cost six pence and three days of lost work. The moot found him a freeholder. The steward accepted the finding with an expression that suggested he considered it temporary. He has kept the moot record.
> **Age 20 (688 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
> **Age 21 (689 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 22 (690 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
> **Age 23 (691 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
> **Age 24 (692 TR)** — The fever lasted three weeks. He spent the fourth week deciding what the experience had changed, if anything. The illness passed without lasting harm.
> **Age 25 (693 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
> **Age 26 (694 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
> **Age 27 (695 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
> **Age 28 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 29 (697 TR)** — The Serolan heard both parties in the chapter house. He was fair in the sense that neither of them got what they wanted. The settlement was binding by custom if not by law and cost him less than a court would have and rather more than he thought the matter was worth.
> **Age 30 (698 TR)** — The illness had stripped him down. He spent months rebuilding, finding it slower than he had expected.
> **Age 31 (699 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
> **Age 32 (700 TR)** — He saw something that was probably his business and kept walking. He thought about it for several weeks.
> **Age 33 (701 TR)** — He made a throw — of a stone, a knife, a horseshoe — that was perfect and that he was never able to repeat. There were witnesses.
> **Age 34 (702 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
> **Age 35 (703 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
> **Age 36 (704 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
> **Age 37 (705 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
> **Age 38 (706 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A grievous wound to the head or face. The damage was permanent — hearing reduced by 3.
> **Age 39 (707 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
> **Age 41 (709 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 42 (710 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
> **Age 43 (711 TR)** — He was certain of something and wrong. He argued his position at length before the evidence became impossible to ignore.
> **Age 44 (712 TR)** — He hung the washing out. It rained within the hour. He took it in again. He did not mention this to anyone.
> **Age 45 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 46 (714 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Selyn Idsen
> **Age 47 (715 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
> **Age 48 (716 TR)** — Something he planted as a throwaway grew into something worth having. He took more care the following year.
> **Age 49 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 50 (718 TR)** — He reached out, or they did. The thing between them was old enough to look smaller than it had. Reconciled with Selyn Idsen
> **Age 51 (719 TR)** — He sat alone watching a fire go down until there was nothing left but ash. He had not intended to sit that long.
> **Age 52 (720 TR)** — It had begun as professional acquaintance. Somewhere in the course of years it became something else — not sentimentally, but in the way that trust accumulates. Selyn Idsen became a close friend

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (685 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
> **Age 19 (686 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.
> **Age 20 (687 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 22 (689 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 23 (690 TR)** — She took her loom to her mother's house and worked from there for a year. The guild does not certify home-weavers; this is an area where the guild's coverage is incomplete by design, because the women who weave at home are not the women the guild is designed to serve. She is not sure whether this is convenient or an insult. She sold everything she produced and used the money to buy better materials. Then she went back to the guild workshop.
> **Age 24 (691 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Morin Dyer
> **Age 25 (692 TR)** — The journey required more of her than she had expected and gave back more than she had hoped.
> **Age 26 (693 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 27 (694 TR)** — She followed a cat through somewhere she did not know and found something she was glad to know was there.
> **Age 28 (695 TR)** — She is the only woman in the workshop. This is a fact with specific texture: not hostile, exactly, but requiring a level of navigation that the male apprentices do not perform. She has worked out what is expected of her in addition to the craft itself — how much social work is required, where the lines are, which of the journeymen are worth the investment and which are looking for a reason to be difficult. She does the work. She also does the extra work. She does not complain about this. Complaining would be a third kind of work.
> **Age 29 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 30 (697 TR)** — She lost a small thing she had carried for years. She did not speak of it but felt its absence for a long time.
> **Age 31 (698 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
> **Age 32 (699 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
> **Age 33 (700 TR)** — The merchant's wife commissioned a piece, was satisfied, commissioned another, and six years later is still commissioning work. This is what a successful practice looks like from the inside: not the exceptional commission but the reliable one, the client who trusts your judgment and whose household requirements are predictable and who pays on time. She has three clients like this. She is working on a fourth. She is aware that all of them are contingent on the merchant's wife continuing to find her work satisfactory, which means she pays attention to every piece she makes for this household as if it is the first one.
> **Age 34 (701 TR)** — She found it by accident. Old coin, mostly, wrapped in rotted cloth. More than she had expected. She told no one for a week.
> **Age 35 (702 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 36 (703 TR)** — She had trusted this person. That trust had been a tool someone else picked up and used. Betrayed by Morin Dyer
> **Age 37 (704 TR)** — The gossip thinned. People forgot, or found newer griefs to occupy them. She was not forgiven so much as left alone.
> **Age 38 (705 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
> **Age 39 (706 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
> **Age 40 (707 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
> **Age 41 (708 TR)** — She fought for what was hers through every channel available to her.
> **Age 42 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 43 (710 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
> **Age 44 (711 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
> **Age 45 (712 TR)** — A fighting order commissioning from a woman was irregular enough that the arms-master who arrived had clearly been told to pretend he did not find it so. She found this easier to work with than direct hostility would have been. The commission was for mail, which is what she does best, and when they came to inspect it, the pretense of indifference became straightforwardly professional. They have sent three subsequent commissions.
> **Age 46 (713 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 47 (714 TR)** — An incident in public damaged her name in the community.
> **Age 48 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 49 (716 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.
> **Age 50 (717 TR)** — Through persistence or her late husband's name, she won a place in the guild.
> **Age 51 (718 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
> **Age 52 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 53 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (684 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
> **Age 19 (685 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Fara Kalsen (unguilded, age 16)
> **Age 20 (686 TR)** — The lord held court in the hall and he was summoned as a party to a dispute about labour obligations. The lord sat in the chair at the end of the hall and heard both sides in the specific way that lords hear disputes: with one eye on the merits and one eye on whether the outcome will produce two functional tenants or one resentful one. The finding went against him on the main point and in his favour on the secondary point, which is a finding designed to ensure that both parties leave with something. He accepted it. The lord's court is not impartial — the lord is also his landlord — but it is not arbitrary either. It is interested, which is a different thing.
> **Age 21 (687 TR)** — The reeve came round in the autumn as he always does, checking the strip yields against the assessment and collecting the lord's share. The assessment this year was wrong — not maliciously wrong, the reeve was not that kind of man, but wrong in the way things are wrong when the person making the assessment has fifteen holdings to assess and not enough days to do it properly. He disputed it at the village meeting. The reeve revised two items. The third item they disagreed about and the reeve's assessment stood. He accepted this. The reeve's assessment standing is not the same as the reeve being right. It means the lord agrees with the reeve, which is a different thing. He will dispute it again next autumn.
> **Age 21 (687 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Ulric Lansen. week 40 Ulric Lansen born (male)
> **Age 22 (688 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A grievous wound to the thigh. The damage was permanent — agility reduced by 2.
> **Age 22 (688 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 23 (689 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
> **Age 23 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 24 (690 TR)** — He told a small lie that had no purpose. The truth would have been fine. He did not know why he had said it.
> **Age 25 (691 TR)** — He noticed the absence of a sound he had not known he was used to. The silence where it should have been was loud.
> **Age 25 (691 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Edda Lansen born (female)
> **Age 26 (692 TR)** — He said something in passing at a market that was repeated and changed and came back to him a month later as a fact he did not recognise.
> **Age 27 (693 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
> **Age 27 (693 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
> **Age 28 (694 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
> **Age 28 (694 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 30
> **Age 29 (695 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
> **Age 29 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 29 (695 TR)** — Halla Lansen died young — a child still. Halla Lansen died (age 6)
> **Age 30 (696 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
> **Age 30 (696 TR)** — The child lived, but it was plain from the first days that something was different. He loved the child completely and did not know what the future held. week 40 — lame Kuric Lansen born (male, lame)
> **Age 31 (697 TR)** — He left in November, which is the worst time to travel and the best time to leave unnoticed — the roads are empty and the lord's reeve does not expect movement. He reached the chartered town in four days and presented himself to the town officer and declared his intent to remain. The year and a day is not a comfortable year. It is a year of knowing that anyone from the home village who recognises you and reports to your lord can end it. He did the work available to him, he stayed within the town boundaries, he did not draw attention. At the end of the year and a day the town officer acknowledged him a freeman. He has never returned to the village. He sends money when he can.
> **Age 31 (697 TR)** — He lay awake thinking about what would happen to the child after he was gone. There was no answer he could land on.
> **Age 32 (698 TR)** — He came home to the door off its hinges and the strongbox open. They had taken the silver plate, his wife's jewellery, and the tools he had been saving for three years. The worst of it was not the loss — it was the knowledge that someone had been through everything, had touched everything, had made themselves at home in his absence.
> **Age 32 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 32 (698 TR)** — Kuric Lansen died — the lame child he had worried over for years. Kuric Lansen died (age 2)
> **Age 33 (699 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
> **Age 33 (699 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
> **Age 34 (700 TR)** — He lent something and it was never returned. He saw the person again and neither of them mentioned it.
> **Age 35 (701 TR)** — He was asked to take sides in a dispute between two neighbours. He tried to remain neutral. Both sides felt he had betrayed them.
> **Age 35 (701 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
> **Age 36 (702 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
> **Age 36 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 37 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 37 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 38 (704 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
> **Age 39 (705 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
> **Age 39 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 40 (706 TR)** — She was gone. A note, or nothing — it did not matter much which. He was left with the children and whatever explanations he could give the neighbours.
> **Age 40 (706 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
> **Age 40 (706 TR)** — Edda Lansen was nearly grown when she died. Edda Lansen died (age 15)
> **Age 41 (707 TR)** — The grain came in heavy. The lord's share still left enough.
> **Age 41 (707 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Kern Lansen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Kern Lansen born (male)
> **Age 41 (707 TR)** — His son Ulric Lansen married Caris Soring. Ulric Lansen married Caris Soring (peasant, age 17)
> **Age 42 (708 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
> **Age 42 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 42 (708 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Ulric Lansen's child born (male, Ulric Lansen age 21)

> **Age 43 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

> **Age 43 (709 TR)** — His daughter Dara Lansen married Arven Borkin. Dara Lansen married Arven Borkin (unguilded, age 18)
> **Age 43 (709 TR)** — Elva Lansen died young — a child still. Elva Lansen died (age 6)
> **Age 44 (710 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
> **Age 44 (710 TR)** — Dara Lansen left home to make his own way. Dara Lansen left home (age 22)

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (683 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
> **Age 19 (684 TR)** — Music was part of a proper upbringing. She took the lessons seriously.
> **Age 20 (685 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (686 TR)** — She thought of what she should have said long after there was any chance to say it.
> **Age 22 (687 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
> **Age 23 (688 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
> **Age 24 (689 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
> **Age 25 (690 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
> **Age 26 (691 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
> **Age 27 (692 TR)** — She heard the midnight hour ring from somewhere she had never been. She lay awake listening to the silence after.
> **Age 28 (693 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
> **Age 29 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 30 (695 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
> **Age 31 (696 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting.
> **Age 32 (697 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 33 (698 TR)** — She was among the crowd when the sentence was carried out. The silence that fell at the end was not what she expected.
> **Age 34 (699 TR)** — She read what texts she could find, and the night sky taught her the rest.
> **Age 35 (700 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
> **Age 36 (701 TR)** — A tune she caught half of at a festival lodged in her head for the rest of the season.
> **Age 37 (702 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 38 (703 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 39 (704 TR)** — She came back to somewhere she had not seen in years. She stood and looked at it for a long time before going in.
> **Age 40 (705 TR)** — She opened her door to someone who needed it.
> **Age 41 (706 TR)** — A deathbed she attended left her with unanswered questions about her own beliefs.
> **Age 42 (707 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Varen
> **Age 43 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 44 (709 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 45 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 46 (711 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.
> **Age 47 (712 TR)** — Matakea. The full title. She had spent seven years as Ashesa working toward this and the ceremony itself was over in an afternoon.
> **Age 48 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> *(Ages 49–50 (714–715 TR): quiet years)*
> **Age 51 (716 TR)** — She was accepted into a noble household as a lady-in-waiting or steward's assistant.
> **Age 52 (717 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Dorin Darekar (noble)
> **Age 53 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 54 (719 TR)** — She found a sealed letter tucked into something she had bought secondhand. She kept it for a year before burning it unread.
> **Age 55 (720 TR)** — A stray dog adopted her without asking. She fed it scraps and pretended it was not her dog.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (682 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
> **Age 19 (683 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.
> **Age 20 (684 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
> **Age 21 (685 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
> **Age 22 (686 TR)** — The watch sergeant was thirty feet away and looking in the wrong direction, which was the correct direction for the watch sergeant to be looking in and the wrong direction for him. He walked at the right pace — not slow enough to look guilty, not fast enough to look fleeing — until he turned the corner and then he walked differently for four blocks before he stopped shaking. He has replayed the thirty feet four hundred times since. He believes he made the right choices. He also believes that the right choices are not sufficient guarantees.
> **Age 23 (687 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
> **Age 24 (688 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 25 (689 TR)** — They came on a Tuesday, two of them — one large, one polite. The polite one explained that the neighbourhood had been experiencing difficulties lately and that a modest arrangement would ensure his shop was not among those affected. He paid. He had a wife and an apprentice. He knew what refusing looked like.
> **Age 26 (690 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
> **Age 28 (692 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
> **Age 29 (693 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the lower leg or shin that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
> **Age 30 (694 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
> **Age 31 (695 TR)** — He counts it three times, as is the rule, and separates it into thirds on the table. The middle third goes into the box. The left third he will deliver tomorrow. The right third is his. He is always slightly surprised that the arithmetic comes out the same.
> **Age 32 (696 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Balar Cargoham (merchant)
> **Age 33 (697 TR)** — The other apprentice got the better tasks, the master's patience, the benefit of the doubt. He got the corrections and the night work. He learned both crafts — the craft itself, and the craft of being the one who is not the favourite.
> **Age 34 (698 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
> **Age 35 (699 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 36 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 37 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 38 (702 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A minor wound to the knee that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
> **Age 39 (703 TR)** — The fence operates from a chandlery in the river quarter and has been in business for twenty years, which in his line of work means either exceptional competence or exceptional luck, and he is not a lucky-looking man. He takes thirty percent, asks no questions, and moves goods within two days. He pays in coin and has never shorted a payment. He is also the kind of person who would inform on you if the price was right and he judged the arrangement was over. He holds this fact about the fence clearly in mind at all times, which is how you maintain a working relationship with a fence.
> **Age 40 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 41 (705 TR)** — He stood in the crowd and watched a man hang. It was meant to be a warning; he was not sure what lesson he took from it.
> **Age 42 (706 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
> **Age 44 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 45 (709 TR)** — He enters every room the same way: two paces in, stop, count the doors and windows. It takes a heartbeat. He has done it so long he no longer thinks about it.
> **Age 46 (710 TR)** — He saw a man die by violence — not on a battlefield, but in the ordinary world: a tavern, a street, a market square. It changed how he looked at strangers.
> **Age 47 (711 TR)** — He had been suspicious for months, and when the pattern became undeniable he brought it to his master rather than acting alone. The informant had been in their cell for three years. The resolution was handled without noise, which was the professional way, and he was commended for not making it a mess.
> **Age 48 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 49 (713 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
> **Age 50 (714 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 51 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 52 (716 TR)** — He walked away from his field and did not look back. The town held something the village could not — or he had no choice but to find out.
> **Age 53 (717 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 55 (719 TR)** — The inquiry was conducted by a knight of the Argent Order — courteous, precise, and entirely certain of what he was looking for. They had received a report. He answered every question honestly and was cleared, which left him with the durable knowledge that someone had put his name before the Order and had done so deliberately.
> **Age 56 (720 TR)** — A lord offered endowment for the chapel and expected pastoral access in return. The arrangement suited both parties and neither was entirely comfortable with it.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (681 TR)** — The woman died in room four. She had arrived alone, which was itself notable. The alderman came the same morning and she walked him through everything she knew: arrival time, manner, payment, whether she had spoken to anyone, whether anyone had enquired after her. The alderman asked whether she thought it was natural causes. She said she did not know and had not formed an opinion and suggested that was his job. The inquiry concluded accidental death. She does not entirely agree with the conclusion but she has said everything she knows and has nothing further to add to the record.
> **Age 19 (682 TR)** — Someone had told the Matakea that she had been seen at a location associated with Navehan activity. She had not been there. The interview was thorough. She was believed, eventually. She spent some time afterward identifying who might have made the report and why.
> **Age 20 (683 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (684 TR)** — The Matakea heard her story and asked no questions about whether it was complete. She was given a room in the lay quarters and told she could stay until she decided what she was doing. That was two weeks, and during that time no one came for her.
> **Age 22 (685 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
> **Age 24 (687 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
> **Age 25 (688 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.
> **Age 26 (689 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.
> **Age 27 (690 TR)** — She rode out with hawk or hound when she could. The forest suited her.
> **Age 28 (691 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
> **Age 29 (692 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
> **Age 31 (694 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 32 (695 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. She would carry it the rest of her life.
> **Age 34 (697 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
> **Age 35 (698 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
> **Age 37 (700 TR)** — The investigation was about whether she was operating a house of assignment in the upper rooms — a serious accusation that, if proven, would cost her the inn charter. She was not. She cooperated fully, allowed a complete inspection, produced the house book going back four years, and invited the alderman to speak with any current or former guest he could locate. The investigation closed with no finding. She knows who made the allegation and why. She has not acted on this knowledge and does not intend to. The record is clear. That is sufficient.
> **Age 38 (701 TR)** — She fought for what was hers through every channel available to her.
> **Age 39 (702 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better.
> **Age 40 (703 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Nara Ering (guilded_innkeeper)
> **Age 41 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 42 (705 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.
> **Age 43 (706 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Mael Valing
> **Age 44 (707 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 45 (708 TR)** — She had learned to be careful. This time she had not been careful enough. The look on the stranger's face told her everything.
> **Age 46 (709 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 47 (710 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.
> **Age 48 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 49 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 51 (714 TR)** — She woke and the cradle was empty. The baby had been there at the third-hour feed. She would never know, in a precise sense, what had happened to her child. She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful.
> **Age 55 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 57 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
