**Age 24 (687 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.

**Age 25 (688 TR)** — The guild expected journeymen to travel and she had obliged, moving on when she had taken what a workshop had to teach. Arriving unknown in a new settlement and finding the master's shop, presenting herself, negotiating her rate — she had done it eleven times and had grown fluent in the particular conversation of a artisan offering her skills to a stranger.

**Age 26 (689 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.
**Age 27 (690 TR)** — She rode out with hawk or hound when she could. The forest suited her.

**Age 28 (691 TR)** — The magistrate heard her case and found against her, which surprised her until she learned what the other party had paid. She had known in the abstract that the law was purchasable in this city. She knew it differently after that.
**Age 29 (692 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
**Age 31 (694 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 32 (695 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. She would carry it the rest of her life.
**Age 34 (697 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.

**Age 35 (698 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.

**Age 37 (700 TR)** — The investigation was about whether she was operating a house of assignment in the upper rooms — a serious accusation that, if proven, would cost her the inn charter. She was not. She cooperated fully, allowed a complete inspection, produced the house book going back four years, and invited the alderman to speak with any current or former guest he could locate. The investigation closed with no finding. She knows who made the allegation and why. She has not acted on this knowledge and does not intend to. The record is clear. That is sufficient.
**Age 38 (701 TR)** — She fought for what was hers through every channel available to her.

**Age 39 (702 TR)** — She does not sit in the common room after the dinner hour. She is present in it — moving between tables, checking the fire, signalling to the kitchen — but she is not stationary in it, because a stationary innkeeper becomes a feature of the room and people stop noticing her, and she wants to be noticed. Not as a presence that changes behaviour, but as a person who is paying attention. The room behaves differently when it knows someone is watching. It behaves better.
**Age 40 (703 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Nara Ering (guilded_innkeeper)
**Age 41 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 42 (705 TR)** — She could tell rain from cloud before most felt the wind shift.
**Age 43 (706 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Mael Valing
**Age 44 (707 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 45 (708 TR)** — She had learned to be careful. This time she had not been careful enough. The look on the stranger's face told her everything.
**Age 46 (709 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.

**Age 47 (710 TR)** — The last guest has been seen to their room. The accounts balance to the Farthing. She is standing in the common room with the fire going low and the room showing what seventeen people did in it today. This is hers: the room, the inn, the daily enterprise of feeding and sheltering and managing the specific parade of humanity that comes through here. She is tired in the way she is always tired at this hour, which is to say: satisfied and ready for tomorrow.
**Age 48 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 49 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 51 (714 TR)** — She woke and the cradle was empty. The baby had been there at the third-hour feed. She would never know, in a precise sense, what had happened to her child. She would spend years constructing theories, each one worse than the last, none of them helpful.
**Age 55 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (680 TR)** — His lord had given an order he could not follow. Not would not — could not, in the sense that following it would have made him someone he did not want to be and could not have looked at in still water. He refused, formally, with witnesses. He was not punished. That surprised him more than the order had.
> **Age 19 (681 TR)** — A secret entrusted to him found its way to those who would pay for it.
> **Age 20 (682 TR)** — He left the range of people who knew his name and spent several months in that condition, which suited him better than expected.
> **Age 21 (683 TR)** — Another family's child came to live under his roof — to learn, to serve as a page, or simply as a pledge of alliance. He found himself responsible for someone else's son. Took in Caris Elendyl as ward
> **Age 22 (684 TR)** — He realised he had been missing something that had been there all along. He was not sure how long he had been missing it.
> **Age 23 (685 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Halla Yaramin (noble, age 23)
> **Age 24 (686 TR)** — He received news that was good, weeks after it would have mattered. He was glad anyway.
> **Age 25 (687 TR)** — The Serolan's man arrived with the assessment and he disagreed with the figure. The disagreement was conducted formally and went nowhere. He paid a revised amount that was still more than he thought just, and the Serolan's man left satisfied in the particular way of someone who has extracted more than they expected.
> **Age 25 (687 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 27
> **Age 26 (688 TR)** — He found his first grey hair. He pulled it out, then felt foolish, then stopped caring.
> **Age 26 (688 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 15
> **Age 27 (689 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
> **Age 28 (690 TR)** — A quarrel turned violent; he left a man bloodied in the dirt.
> **Age 28 (690 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 13
> **Age 29 (691 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Kael Zarekar
> **Age 29 (691 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Yoric Yaramin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Yoric Yaramin born (male)
> **Age 30 (692 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
> **Age 30 (692 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Calla Yaramin. week 40 Calla Yaramin born (female)
> **Age 30 (692 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Yoric Yaramin died (age 1)
> **Age 31 (693 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Trovan Naramin died
> **Age 31 (693 TR)** — His wife lost the child early. Neither of them spoke much about it. week 7
> **Age 32 (694 TR)** — The Matakea walked the line in the hour before dawn, touching each soldier's shoulder. He had heard the Alamirata a hundred times in temple. Here it meant something different — the same words, the same cadence, but spoken over men who might be dead by midday. He had not expected to find it comforting. He found it comforting.
> **Age 32 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 33 (695 TR)** — Figures were altered, documents forged — profit at the cost of someone else's ruin.
> **Age 33 (695 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
> **Age 34 (696 TR)** — He was taken. Ransom, eventually, freed him — but the months in captivity left a mark. A minor wound to the forearm or hand that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
> **Age 34 (696 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 35 (697 TR)** — It had not started as enmity. By the end, it was nothing else. Caris Elendyl became an enemy
> **Age 35 (697 TR)** — One of his children brought home an animal no one had agreed to keep. Everyone pretended not to be fond of it. It stayed for years.
> **Age 36 (698 TR)** — The accounts and obligations of the estate fell to him.
> **Age 36 (698 TR)** — The pregnancy ended before it had properly begun. He did not know what to say, so he said nothing useful. week 4
> **Age 37 (699 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A minor wound to the head or face that healed cleanly, leaving only a scar.
> **Age 37 (699 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Ranulf Yaramin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Ranulf Yaramin born (male)
> **Age 37 (699 TR)** — Calla Yaramin died young — a child still. Calla Yaramin died (age 7)
> **Age 38 (700 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it.
> **Age 38 (700 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Breda Yaramin — survived. week 40 Breda Yaramin born (female)
> **Age 39 (701 TR)** — A tutor, a priest, or simply a talented neighbour opened music to him.
> **Age 39 (701 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
> **Age 40 (702 TR)** — He said nothing when he very much wanted to say something. He was right not to. He did not feel better for being right.
> **Age 41 (703 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
> **Age 42 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 43 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 44 (706 TR)** — He followed a cat through an unfamiliar part of town on a whim and ended up somewhere he would not otherwise have found.
> **Age 44 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 45 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 45 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 46 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 46 (708 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
> **Age 47 (709 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
> **Age 47 (709 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
> **Age 48 (710 TR)** — His father was dead and the land was his. Grief and obligation arrived together.
> **Age 48 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 49 (711 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the forearm or hand. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in dexterity (−1).
> **Age 49 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 50 (712 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
> **Age 50 (712 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
> **Age 51 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 51 (713 TR)** — Halla Yaramin died. They had been married 28 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Halla Yaramin died (approx. age 51)
> **Age 52 (714 TR)** — Lineage mattered. He learned to read shields and know their histories.
> **Age 52 (714 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.
> **Age 53 (715 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
> **Age 53 (715 TR)** — His son Ranulf Yaramin married Ulda Yaramin. Ranulf Yaramin married Ulda Yaramin (noble, age 16)
> **Age 54 (716 TR)** — The Argent Order knights were not what the stories suggested — they were quieter, more methodical, and considerably more frightening than the stories suggested. He served under their command for six weeks and learned more about the Navehan church than he had wanted to know. Their discipline was absolute. They did not celebrate kills.
> **Age 55 (717 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
> **Age 55 (717 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Ranulf Yaramin's child born (female, Ranulf Yaramin age 18)
> **Age 55 (717 TR)** — Ranulf Yaramin left home to make his own way. Ranulf Yaramin left home (age 18)
> **Age 55 (717 TR)** — His daughter Breda Yaramin married Ranulf Joiner. Breda Yaramin married Ranulf Joiner (artisan, age 16)
> **Age 56 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 56 (718 TR)** — Ranulf Yaramin died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Ranulf Yaramin died (age 19)
> **Age 56 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 56 (718 TR)** — Breda Yaramin left home to make his own way. Breda Yaramin left home (age 18)
> **Age 57 (719 TR)** — He got caught in the rain far from shelter. By the time he gave up and stood still, he was already soaked. He let it finish.
> **Age 58 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 58 (720 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.

**Age 37 (699 TR)** — Calla Yaramin died young — a child still. Calla Yaramin died (age 7)

**Age 38 (700 TR)** — The child had been burning for three days when the Peoni sister came. She said little, worked steadily, and left instructions that his wife followed exactly. By the end of the week the fever was gone. He made a donation to the temple that he could not really afford and did not regret it.
**Age 38 (700 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Breda Yaramin — survived. week 40 Breda Yaramin born (female)
**Age 39 (701 TR)** — A tutor, a priest, or simply a talented neighbour opened music to him.
**Age 39 (701 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 40 (702 TR)** — He said nothing when he very much wanted to say something. He was right not to. He did not feel better for being right.
**Age 41 (703 TR)** — The saddle became as natural as walking.
**Age 42 (704 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 44 (706 TR)** — He followed a cat through an unfamiliar part of town on a whim and ended up somewhere he would not otherwise have found.
**Age 44 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 45 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 45 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 46 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 46 (708 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
**Age 47 (709 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
**Age 47 (709 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 48 (710 TR)** — His father was dead and the land was his. Grief and obligation arrived together.
**Age 48 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (711 TR)** — The wound that had seemed healed reminded him it was still there. A serious wound to the forearm or hand. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in dexterity (−1).
**Age 49 (711 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 50 (712 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
**Age 50 (712 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
**Age 51 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 51 (713 TR)** — Halla Yaramin died. They had been married 28 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Halla Yaramin died (approx. age 51)
**Age 52 (714 TR)** — Lineage mattered. He learned to read shields and know their histories.
**Age 52 (714 TR)** — His child decided to do something — cook, chop, mend — without being asked. The result was memorable. He cleaned it up without making them feel bad.

**Age 53 (715 TR)** — The Argent Order knight identified the man as Navehan, which explained several things about the last two weeks. He killed him in the tunnel mouth with a hand-axe in less than thirty seconds. The killing felt different from others he had done. Not better, exactly — but more contained, more purposeful. He did not examine this feeling very closely.
**Age 53 (715 TR)** — His son Ranulf Yaramin married Ulda Yaramin. Ranulf Yaramin married Ulda Yaramin (noble, age 16)

**Age 54 (716 TR)** — The Argent Order knights were not what the stories suggested — they were quieter, more methodical, and considerably more frightening than the stories suggested. He served under their command for six weeks and learned more about the Navehan church than he had wanted to know. Their discipline was absolute. They did not celebrate kills.
**Age 55 (717 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 55 (717 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Ranulf Yaramin's child born (female, Ranulf Yaramin age 18)
**Age 55 (717 TR)** — Ranulf Yaramin left home to make his own way. Ranulf Yaramin left home (age 18)
**Age 55 (717 TR)** — His daughter Breda Yaramin married Ranulf Joiner. Breda Yaramin married Ranulf Joiner (artisan, age 16)
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — Ranulf Yaramin died. He had not thought a parent could grieve an adult child as much as an infant. He had been wrong. Ranulf Yaramin died (age 19)
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (718 TR)** — Breda Yaramin left home to make his own way. Breda Yaramin left home (age 18)
**Age 57 (719 TR)** — He got caught in the rain far from shelter. By the time he gave up and stood still, he was already soaked. He let it finish.
**Age 58 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 58 (720 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (679 TR)** — Something she had been looking for was where it had always been. She had simply not been looking at the right place.
> **Age 19 (680 TR)** — The venture soured. Creditors grew impatient.
> **Age 20 (681 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
> **Age 21 (682 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
> **Age 22 (683 TR)** — The venture soured. Creditors grew impatient.
> **Age 23 (684 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
> **Age 24 (685 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
> **Age 25 (686 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 26 (687 TR)** — She heard a priest say something she thought was wrong and kept her opinion to herself. She debated this decision for some time.
> **Age 27 (688 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
> **Age 28 (689 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
> **Age 29 (690 TR)** — She waited in line. She was next. The thing she had been waiting for was gone.
> **Age 30 (691 TR)** — Brigands relieved her of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 31 (692 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Voran Dustmantle
> **Age 32 (693 TR)** — She had trusted this person. That trust had been a tool someone else picked up and used. Betrayed by Voran Dustmantle
> **Age 33 (694 TR)** — When the coin was offered, she found reasons to look the other way.
> **Age 34 (695 TR)** — The tithe demand had been adjusted upward without explanation. She queried it in writing, which was her right, and received a written response explaining that the adjustment was correct. She paid it. She did not forget it.
> **Age 35 (696 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Yorin Finecoin
> **Age 36 (697 TR)** — __colour__
> **Age 37 (698 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
> **Age 38 (699 TR)** — The debt that had dogged her was finally cleared.
> **Age 39 (700 TR)** — The venture soured. Creditors grew impatient.
> **Age 40 (701 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Tala Westmarket (merchant)
> **Age 41 (702 TR)** — She watched the web of favours and enmities carefully, learning where to place her feet.
> **Age 42 (703 TR)** — Figures were altered, documents forged — profit at the cost of someone else's ruin.
> **Age 43 (704 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
> **Age 44 (705 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Kael Packworth
> **Age 45 (706 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 46 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 47 (708 TR)** — She had expected the priest to favour the other party, who was male and of better standing. He did not. She received a settlement that was just if not generous and was left with a more complicated view of the church than she had arrived with.
> **Age 49 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 50 (711 TR)** — The old woman had been slowly handing over more of the work for three years. When she died, the guild transfer was contested by a cousin who knew nothing of the trade. She fought it through the Mangai and won on the merits. She paid the inheritance fee in full rather than in instalments, which she had not quite been able to afford, and which she did not regret.
> **Age 51 (712 TR)** — The Ebasethe arrived before dawn and stayed until the crisis passed. She had sent for her because the midwife said to. She had not expected it to matter. The child was alive. She had a different understanding of the Peonian church after that — less abstract, more personal.
> **Age 52 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 53 (714 TR)** — The approach was made in person, by a woman she had never seen, who knew things about her that no one had any business knowing. The price was stated plainly. She paid it. She is still paying it. She has not yet determined who is behind the approach, which tells her they are very good at this.
> **Age 54 (715 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 55 (716 TR)** — She bought cheap and sold dear, and the ledger proved it.
> **Age 56 (717 TR)** — She misjudged the ground and arrived somewhere in a condition that was difficult to explain.
> **Age 57 (718 TR)** — She had thought herself a better judge of odds than she was. The coin was gone before she had fully reckoned what she'd risked.
> **Age 58 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 59 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (678 TR)** — Drink, pride, or an old grudge turned into something harder. He gave as good as he got.
> **Age 19 (679 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Arnam Arson
> **Age 20 (680 TR)** — Loss drove him inward; he spent months in solitary reflection.
> **Age 21 (681 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
> **Age 22 (682 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
> **Age 23 (683 TR)** — The journey took him far enough from home that home became an abstraction he could think about rather than simply inhabit.
> **Age 24 (684 TR)** — He reached out, or they did. The thing between them was old enough to look smaller than it had. Reconciled with Arnam Arson
> **Age 25 (685 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Arith Elkin (warrior, age 22)
> **Age 26 (686 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
> **Age 27 (687 TR)** — Three priests, four acolytes, six guardsmen. They were told about the feigned conversions afterward, by the sole survivor who had been away for supplies. He had memorised the number. It was the kind of number that explained an entire policy.
> **Age 27 (687 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
> **Age 28 (688 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
> **Age 29 (689 TR)** — He noticed, around the middle of the year, that things had been going well. He decided not to think about this too much.
> **Age 29 (689 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
> **Age 30 (690 TR)** — He did not handle the loss publicly. He handled it alone, over a long time, in ways that left marks.
> **Age 30 (690 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Roric Borkin. week 40 Roric Borkin born (male)
> **Age 31 (691 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
> **Age 31 (691 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 25
> **Age 32 (692 TR)** — Named outlaw by a lord or court. Travel to markets and towns carried new risk.
> **Age 32 (692 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Britta Borkin born (female)
> **Age 32 (692 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Roric Borkin died (age 2)
> **Age 33 (693 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 33 (693 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 34 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 34 (694 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Orla Borkin came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Orla Borkin born (female)
> **Age 34 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 35 (695 TR)** — He was posted to the garrison watching the Osel crossing. The first month he did not understand why everyone kept one hand near a weapon at all times. By the third month he understood.
> **Age 35 (695 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 36 (696 TR)** — The years finally told. He moved more carefully, tired more quickly. The illness caused serious permanent damage: strength −3, stamina −1.
> **Age 36 (696 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
> **Age 37 (697 TR)** — The Matakea had gone down fifty yards out and the line was already pulling back. He did not make a decision, precisely — he moved before he had decided to move and the thinking happened afterward. He got her back to the line. She was dead. He had not known she was dead when he went for her. He has not entirely resolved how he feels about that.
> **Age 38 (698 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
> **Age 38 (698 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
> **Age 39 (699 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
> **Age 39 (699 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 40 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 40 (700 TR)** — Something happened — he could never explain what to outsiders — that had the whole household laughing until they couldn't stand. He still smiled at the memory years later.
> **Age 41 (701 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
> **Age 41 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 42 (702 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
> **Age 43 (703 TR)** — Something struck him as funny at completely the wrong moment. He was unable to stop laughing for far longer than the situation called for.
> **Age 43 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

> **Age 46 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 46 (706 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

> **Age 48 (708 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 16

> **Age 58 (718 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.

**Age 56 (716 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 57 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 57 (717 TR)** — Orla Borkin left home to make his own way. Orla Borkin left home (age 23)
**Age 57 (717 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Orlan Borkin's child born (female, Orlan Borkin age 22)

**Age 58 (718 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 58 (718 TR)** — His daughter Sira Borkin married Halric Waling. Sira Borkin married Halric Waling (warrior, age 17)
**Age 59 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (719 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (719 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Yorin Borkin's illegitimate child born (male, Yorin Borkin age 16)
**Age 60 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (677 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Dera Packworth (merchant)
> **Age 19 (678 TR)** — She was certain of the weather and entirely mistaken. She said nothing about it. Others said enough.
> **Age 20 (679 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
> **Age 21 (680 TR)** — The camp economy runs on the sutlers and everyone knows it, including the officers who would prefer not to acknowledge it. She extends credit carefully — who is reliable, who has family money, who is the kind of man who runs when things go wrong. She has been cheated precisely twice and recovered from both by knowing which sergeant to speak to. She is carrying the wages of four dead men in her ledger, which she will attempt to recover from their estates when the campaign ends, and which she is fairly certain she will not recover.
> **Age 22 (681 TR)** — She was asked to deliver a package to a specific house in a town sixty miles out and note, on the way back, what she could observe about the movement of men and horses on the Tashal road. She delivered the package. She observed the movement. Her report contained three pieces of information her officer had not expected, which she knows because of how he reacted to those three pieces. She is aware she is useful in a specific way. She is deciding what to do with that awareness.
> **Age 23 (682 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Rhoric Oring (soldier, age 27)
> **Age 24 (683 TR)** — She was not trained for battlefield wounds. She was trained for illness, for fever, for childbirth. She adapted in the first campaign and has been adapting since. The military surgeons, when there are any, do not always know more than she does. Sometimes they know less. She does the work that needs doing and has stopped spending energy on the question of whether she is supposed to be the one doing it.
> **Age 25 (684 TR)** — She learned to present herself as more useful than she was on any given day, because soldiers who appear less useful than the cost of feeding them get reassigned in ways that are worse. She learned to read the officer's mood at fifty yards and adjust accordingly. She learned which medical knowledge was actually useful in the field and which was the kind of thing you learned in training to feel prepared. The gap between those two categories is significant.
> **Age 25 (684 TR)** — The child came too still. She had carried it through the quickening, had felt it move, and then nothing. week 27
> **Age 26 (685 TR)** — She is a widow visiting her sister's family, which is why she is on this road, which is why she has stopped at this inn, which is why she is asking about the road ahead to the next village. None of this is true. All of it holds. She has learned that the most sustainable cover is one that requires the minimum number of lies — the one that lets the truth do as much of the work as possible.
> **Age 27 (686 TR)** — She kept the household alive through ingenuity and stubbornness.
> **Age 27 (686 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
> **Age 28 (687 TR)** — She woke knowing what she needed to do. She had gone to sleep with no idea. She did not question it.
> **Age 28 (687 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Britta Oring — lived. week 40 Britta Oring born (female)
> **Age 29 (688 TR)** — __colour__
> **Age 29 (688 TR)** — Her child carried something home — a dog, a cat, a bird with a broken wing — that no one had said yes to. It lived in the house for a long time.
> **Age 30 (689 TR)** — The cover held but it was close. A merchant's wife asked her about a village she claimed to be from and knew it in a level of detail she didn't — specific names, specific streets. She deflected smoothly enough but the deflection was noticed; she saw it noticed. She completed the task and returned and asked her officer directly whether she needed to change the cover identity. He said not yet. She is keeping very careful track of what 'not yet' means in practice.
> **Age 30 (689 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 30 (689 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Rara Oring died (age 0)
> **Age 31 (690 TR)** — She held back what she wanted to say for too long. By the time she was ready to say it, the occasion had closed.
> **Age 31 (690 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
> **Age 32 (691 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 32 (691 TR)** — Her husband snored like something structural was collapsing. She learned to sleep through it. She never told him how long this took.
> **Age 33 (692 TR)** — There is a specific kind of problem that comes with being a woman in a company that has not fully decided how to handle women in a company. She handled it. She is not going to describe in detail how she handled it because the details are not the point — the point is that she handled it in a way that did not require the sergeant's intervention and did not damage her operational standing and produced a working arrangement with the person involved that, while not friendly, is functional. She has added this to her list of skills that are not in any training manual.
> **Age 33 (692 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
> **Age 34 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 35 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 35 (694 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Ceris Oring. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Ceris Oring born (female)
> **Age 36 (695 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
> **Age 36 (695 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Wela Oring lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Wela Oring born (female)
> **Age 37 (696 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
> **Age 37 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 38 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 38 (697 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
> **Age 39 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 39 (698 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 20
> **Age 40 (699 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
> **Age 40 (699 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Alara Oring lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Alara Oring born (female)
> **Age 41 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 41 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 42 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 42 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 43 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 43 (702 TR)** — Rhoric Oring died after 20 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Rhoric Oring died (approx. age 47)
> **Age 43 (702 TR)** — She lost Alara Oring — still a child, not yet grown. Alara Oring died (age 3)
> **Age 44 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 44 (703 TR)** — Her daughter Britta Oring married Talar Morkin. Britta Oring married Talar Morkin (soldier, age 16)
> **Age 45 (704 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 46 (705 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
> **Age 47 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 48 (707 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A grievous wound to the shoulder. The damage was permanent — strength reduced by 3.
> **Age 48 (707 TR)** — Britta Oring left to make her own way. Britta Oring left home (age 20)
> **Age 49 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 49 (708 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Britta Oring's child born (male, Britta Oring age 21)
> **Age 50 (709 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.
> **Age 51 (710 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively.
> **Age 51 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 52 (711 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
> **Age 52 (711 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
> **Age 52 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 52 (711 TR)** — Her daughter Ceris Oring married Marek Borson. Ceris Oring married Marek Borson (soldier, age 16)
> **Age 53 (712 TR)** — She promised to do something she expected to regret. Keeping it cost her, and the cost was worth it.
> **Age 53 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 53 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 54 (713 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Davin Waling died
> **Age 54 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 55 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 56 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 56 (715 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
> **Age 57 (716 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 57 (716 TR)** — Ceris Oring left to make her own way. Ceris Oring left home (age 22)
> **Age 58 (717 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Dera Packworth has died
> **Age 58 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 58 (717 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Kara Oring's illegitimate child born (male, Kara Oring age 16)
> **Age 59 (718 TR)** — She repaired something repeatedly that had been repaired too many times already. She was not sure why she did not let it go.
> **Age 59 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 60 (719 TR)** — The vow was made in private, to the sword-altar, in language she had not planned and could not quite reproduce afterward. She survived. She has been paying the debt ever since in ways she has not always been able to name — small acts, a certain quality of attention, a refusal of one particular easy choice, repeated.
> **Age 60 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 60 (719 TR)** — Britta Oring lost Evan Oring. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Britta Oring's child died (age 8)
> **Age 61 (720 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
> **Age 61 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 61 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 61 (720 TR)** — Kara Oring lost Zoric Oring. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Kara Oring's child died (age 3)

**Age 34 (693 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 35 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 35 (694 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Ceris Oring. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Ceris Oring born (female)
**Age 36 (695 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
**Age 36 (695 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Wela Oring lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Wela Oring born (female)
**Age 37 (696 TR)** — Necessity had long since made her skilled. She went beyond necessity.
**Age 37 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 38 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 38 (697 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
**Age 39 (698 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 39 (698 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 20
**Age 40 (699 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
**Age 40 (699 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Alara Oring lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Alara Oring born (female)
**Age 41 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 42 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 42 (701 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 43 (702 TR)** — Rhoric Oring died after 20 years together. The silence in the house that followed was unlike any other silence. Rhoric Oring died (approx. age 47)
**Age 43 (702 TR)** — She lost Alara Oring — still a child, not yet grown. Alara Oring died (age 3)
**Age 44 (703 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 44 (703 TR)** — Her daughter Britta Oring married Talar Morkin. Britta Oring married Talar Morkin (soldier, age 16)
**Age 45 (704 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.

**Age 46 (705 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
**Age 47 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (707 TR)** — An accident or fall left her with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A grievous wound to the shoulder. The damage was permanent — strength reduced by 3.
**Age 48 (707 TR)** — Britta Oring left to make her own way. Britta Oring left home (age 20)
**Age 49 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 49 (708 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Britta Oring's child born (male, Britta Oring age 21)
**Age 50 (709 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.

**Age 51 (710 TR)** — She identified it as camp fever on the second day and began the isolation protocols that the army officer had not heard of and was initially resistant to. She explained clearly why isolation was necessary. He allowed it after two days. Those two days probably cost two of the seven men who died. She has thought about how to explain this more quickly next time, which is the productive direction for the thought, and she returns to it when she is not too tired to think productively.
**Age 51 (710 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.

**Age 52 (711 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
**Age 52 (711 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
**Age 52 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 52 (711 TR)** — Her daughter Ceris Oring married Marek Borson. Ceris Oring married Marek Borson (soldier, age 16)
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — She promised to do something she expected to regret. Keeping it cost her, and the cost was worth it.
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 54 (713 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Davin Waling died
**Age 54 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 55 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 56 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (715 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
**Age 57 (716 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
**Age 57 (716 TR)** — Ceris Oring left to make her own way. Ceris Oring left home (age 22)
**Age 58 (717 TR)** — She heard of the death through others — there had been no warning, no leave-taking. Dera Packworth has died
**Age 58 (717 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (717 TR)** — One of her children had a child outside marriage. Kara Oring's illegitimate child born (male, Kara Oring age 16)
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — She repaired something repeatedly that had been repaired too many times already. She was not sure why she did not let it go.
**Age 59 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 60 (719 TR)** — The vow was made in private, to the sword-altar, in language she had not planned and could not quite reproduce afterward. She survived. She has been paying the debt ever since in ways she has not always been able to name — small acts, a certain quality of attention, a refusal of one particular easy choice, repeated.
**Age 60 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 60 (719 TR)** — Britta Oring lost Evan Oring. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Britta Oring's child died (age 8)

**Age 61 (720 TR)** — She wrote off four debts this campaign. Two dead, one deserted, one simply gone. She adjusts the prices for everyone else accordingly, which is the honest way to run a sutler's operation: the cost of unrecoverable debt is distributed across the credit that does get repaid. She does not complain about this. It is a known cost of the trade. She does note the names carefully, because some of the deserters end up in other armies, and the road network is smaller than it appears.
**Age 61 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 61 (720 TR)** — Kara Oring lost Zoric Oring. She watched her child grieve and did not know how to carry both sorrows at once. Kara Oring's child died (age 3)
