> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (676 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
> **Age 19 (677 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
> **Age 20 (678 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
> **Age 21 (679 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
> **Age 22 (680 TR)** — He spent a season helping a craftsman, learning more than he expected.
> **Age 23 (681 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
> **Age 24 (682 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
> **Age 25 (683 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
> **Age 26 (684 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 27 (685 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
> **Age 28 (686 TR)** — He said something he had intended only for one person at a volume that reached several others. He is still not certain how much they heard.
> **Age 29 (687 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Pedar Edson (peasant)
> **Age 30 (688 TR)** — He was caught in a small lie — not an important one, but it was noticed and the noticing was worse than the lie.
> **Age 31 (689 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Erith Joiner (artisan, age 25)
> **Age 31 (689 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 11
> **Age 32 (690 TR)** — Circumstances put him outside for the night with no shelter. The sky was clear. He slept reasonably well and was stiff in the morning.
> **Age 32 (690 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
> **Age 33 (691 TR)** — He made a throw — of a stone, a knife, a horseshoe — that was perfect and that he was never able to repeat. There were witnesses.
> **Age 33 (691 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.
> **Age 34 (692 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
> **Age 34 (692 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
> **Age 35 (693 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
> **Age 35 (693 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 30
> **Age 36 (694 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
> **Age 36 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 37 (695 TR)** — He left in November, which is the worst time to travel and the best time to leave unnoticed — the roads are empty and the lord's reeve does not expect movement. He reached the chartered town in four days and presented himself to the town officer and declared his intent to remain. The year and a day is not a comfortable year. It is a year of knowing that anyone from the home village who recognises you and reports to your lord can end it. He did the work available to him, he stayed within the town boundaries, he did not draw attention. At the end of the year and a day the town officer acknowledged him a freeman. He has never returned to the village. He sends money when he can.
> **Age 37 (695 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
> **Age 38 (696 TR)** — The charges did not stick — lack of evidence, a well-placed word, or simple luck.
> **Age 38 (696 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Lucan Corsen born (male)
> **Age 39 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 39 (697 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Hrolf Corsen — survived. week 40 Hrolf Corsen born (male)
> **Age 40 (698 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
> **Age 40 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 41 (699 TR)** — He heard something outside in the night, got up to investigate, and found nothing. He went back to bed and lay awake for an hour.
> **Age 42 (700 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the shoulder. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in strength (−1).
> **Age 42 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 43 (701 TR)** — He fixed something that had been broken for years, that everyone had simply learned to work around. No one had asked him to.
> **Age 43 (701 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
> **Age 44 (702 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 20
> **Age 45 (703 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
> **Age 45 (703 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Balar Corsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Balar Corsen born (male)
> **Age 46 (704 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
> **Age 46 (704 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
> **Age 46 (704 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Balar Corsen died (age 1)
> **Age 47 (705 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
> **Age 47 (705 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Hrolf Corsen hired out (age 8)
> **Age 48 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 48 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 49 (707 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.
> **Age 50 (708 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
> **Age 50 (708 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.
> **Age 51 (709 TR)** — The face at the edge of the market. He is almost certain. The same way of moving — unhurried, too unhurried, watching without watching. He keeps his hand on his purse and decides not to decide anything yet.
> **Age 51 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 52 (710 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
> **Age 52 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 53 (711 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.
> **Age 53 (711 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
> **Age 54 (712 TR)** — A disagreement that should have been small became something permanent. He was not sure who was more at fault. Fell out with Gerin Goldcloak
> **Age 55 (713 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
> **Age 55 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 56 (714 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 56 (714 TR)** — Erith Joiner died. They had been married 25 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Erith Joiner died (approx. age 50)
> **Age 57 (715 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. He noted the amount in his accounts under the heading of the lord's right to do this being explicitly acknowledged in the terms of his tenure. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop.
> **Age 57 (715 TR)** — His son Lucan Corsen married Arwen Valkin. Lucan Corsen married Arwen Valkin (peasant, age 17)
> **Age 57 (715 TR)** — His son Durst Corsen married Kelyn Warkin. Durst Corsen married Kelyn Warkin (peasant, age 16)
> **Age 58 (716 TR)** — Word reached him that someone he had known was gone. He found he minded more than he expected. Pedar Edson has died
> **Age 58 (716 TR)** — His son Hrolf Corsen married Vala Wheeler. Hrolf Corsen married Vala Wheeler (artisan, age 16)
> **Age 58 (716 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Hrolf Corsen's child born (female, Hrolf Corsen age 19)
> **Age 59 (717 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
> **Age 59 (717 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
> **Age 60 (718 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
> **Age 60 (718 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.
> **Age 61 (719 TR)** — He looked around at a feast and realised he knew almost no one his own age. The men he had grown up with, trained with, quarrelled with — most of them were gone. He was still here. He was not sure what that meant.
> **Age 62 (720 TR)** — Through hard work and some luck, the last creditor was satisfied.
> **Age 62 (720 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
narrative: **Age 18 (676 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
**Age 19 (677 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 20 (678 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
**Age 21 (679 TR)** — An incident in public damaged his name in the community.
**Age 22 (680 TR)** — He spent a season helping a craftsman, learning more than he expected.
**Age 23 (681 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 24 (682 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
**Age 25 (683 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
**Age 26 (684 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 27 (685 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
**Age 28 (686 TR)** — He said something he had intended only for one person at a volume that reached several others. He is still not certain how much they heard.
**Age 29 (687 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Pedar Edson (peasant)
**Age 30 (688 TR)** — He was caught in a small lie — not an important one, but it was noticed and the noticing was worse than the lie.
**Age 31 (689 TR)** — He took a wife, binding two households together. Married Erith Joiner (artisan, age 25)
**Age 31 (689 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 11
**Age 32 (690 TR)** — Circumstances put him outside for the night with no shelter. The sky was clear. He slept reasonably well and was stiff in the morning.
**Age 32 (690 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
**Age 33 (691 TR)** — He made a throw — of a stone, a knife, a horseshoe — that was perfect and that he was never able to repeat. There were witnesses.
**Age 33 (691 TR)** — His wife began to snore. He discovered he could sleep through it after a few months. This became a thing he was quietly proud of.

**Age 34 (692 TR)** — The royal forester caught him with a deer on royal forest land. He is a first offender, which is the only good part of this. First offenders are beaten — formally, by the forester's men, which is different from being beaten informally in that it is recorded and witnessed and the number of strokes is specified and he can stand in front of anyone afterward and say it was done correctly under the law. He accepted it without resistance because resistance is how a first offence becomes a hanging offence. He is not going back to the royal forest. The deer are not worth it now that he knows exactly what the foresters are willing to do to a man they catch alone in the wood.
**Age 34 (692 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
**Age 35 (693 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
**Age 35 (693 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 30
**Age 36 (694 TR)** — The crop rotted or the rains came late. Winter would be lean.
**Age 36 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 37 (695 TR)** — He left in November, which is the worst time to travel and the best time to leave unnoticed — the roads are empty and the lord's reeve does not expect movement. He reached the chartered town in four days and presented himself to the town officer and declared his intent to remain. The year and a day is not a comfortable year. It is a year of knowing that anyone from the home village who recognises you and reports to your lord can end it. He did the work available to him, he stayed within the town boundaries, he did not draw attention. At the end of the year and a day the town officer acknowledged him a freeman. He has never returned to the village. He sends money when he can.

**Age 37 (695 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 38 (696 TR)** — The charges did not stick — lack of evidence, a well-placed word, or simple luck.
**Age 38 (696 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Lucan Corsen born (male)
**Age 39 (697 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 39 (697 TR)** — His wife's labour was hard, and she was weakened for months. The child — Hrolf Corsen — survived. week 40 Hrolf Corsen born (male)

**Age 40 (698 TR)** — The hundred moot is held monthly and most months he has no business there. This month he does — a minor dispute about a field boundary that has been running since before he inherited the holding. He has the documentation. The other party also has documentation. The moot will look at both sets of documentation and reach a finding. He is not certain the finding will favour him. He has prepared an appeal to the shire assize on the assumption it won't, which is not pessimism but preparation. The moot is a public proceeding and everyone in the room knows everyone else and their business. This is simultaneously the moot's strength and its limitation.
**Age 40 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 41 (699 TR)** — He heard something outside in the night, got up to investigate, and found nothing. He went back to bed and lay awake for an hour.
**Age 42 (700 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the shoulder. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in strength (−1).
**Age 42 (700 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — He fixed something that had been broken for years, that everyone had simply learned to work around. No one had asked him to.
**Age 43 (701 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
**Age 44 (702 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 20

**Age 45 (703 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
**Age 45 (703 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Balar Corsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Balar Corsen born (male)
**Age 46 (704 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.

**Age 46 (704 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
**Age 46 (704 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Balar Corsen died (age 1)

**Age 47 (705 TR)** — He had the broadsword because he found it, which is not a defence. He carried it for two months because he needed a sword and it was a good sword and he told himself no one would notice. Someone noticed. He was brought before the hundred moot on a charge of breach of rank: carrying chivalric arms without the legal right to do so. The moot fined him, confiscated the sword, and issued a formal warning that a repeat offence would carry corporal punishment. He paid the fine. He has carried a shortsword since, which is his right, and which he is careful to carry visibly so that there is no question about what it is.
**Age 47 (705 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Hrolf Corsen hired out (age 8)
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 48 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 49 (707 TR)** — Necessity made him the nearest thing to a healer in his community.

**Age 50 (708 TR)** — The shout went up from the market quarter and he was close enough that joining was not optional — the custom is that any man who hears it and does not join is liable to be treated as an accomplice. He ran with the others for three streets before they cornered the man in a yard. The man had stolen a purse. He held one arm while the officers bound him. The whole thing lasted perhaps ten minutes. He thought about it afterward: the specific way the crowd becomes a legal instrument when the right words are shouted, and how different it felt to be part of that instrument than he had expected it to feel.
**Age 50 (708 TR)** — His child fell into the river. He went in after them. Everyone was fine. He did not stop shaking until the next morning.

**Age 51 (709 TR)** — The face at the edge of the market. He is almost certain. The same way of moving — unhurried, too unhurried, watching without watching. He keeps his hand on his purse and decides not to decide anything yet.
**Age 51 (709 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 52 (710 TR)** — He was called to the hundred assize as a juror — yeoman tenure creates this obligation and he has known it was coming since he was granted the holding. Three days of cases: a land boundary dispute, two debt matters, a man accused of breaking into a mill. He listened to each case and the testimony of witnesses and asked the questions the court procedure allowed him to ask and formed judgments. The judgment in the mill case he is less certain of than the others. The man was probably guilty but the testimony was inconsistent. He voted with the majority. He is still thinking about the inconsistency.
**Age 52 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 53 (711 TR)** — His father died and the lord's right to heriot was immediate: the best beast, or a cash equivalent if you preferred to keep the beast. He paid in cash. The amount was calculated by the reeve against the estate valuation and he disputed two of the three items in the valuation and settled the third because it was correct. The payment took everything he had saved in the previous two years. He is now the effective head of the family holding and has begun saving again. The death tax on a freeman's estate is not technically death — it is the lord's way of reminding you that the arrangement is contractual and the contract has terms.

**Age 53 (711 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
**Age 54 (712 TR)** — A disagreement that should have been small became something permanent. He was not sure who was more at fault. Fell out with Gerin Goldcloak
**Age 55 (713 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 55 (713 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
**Age 56 (714 TR)** — Erith Joiner died. They had been married 25 years. He had not thought enough about what that meant until it was over. Erith Joiner died (approx. age 50)

**Age 57 (715 TR)** — His daughter's marriage required the lord's approval and the lord's approval required the merchet payment — five per cent of the annual holding value, which is the customary rate and which is not negotiable. He paid it without dispute because disputing it is futile and the marriage was worth having. This is one of the ways in which a man who holds land under a lord does not quite own it. He has been thinking about the distinction between tenure and ownership since his father explained it to him. He has not found the thinking productive but he has not been able to stop.
**Age 57 (715 TR)** — His son Lucan Corsen married Arwen Valkin. Lucan Corsen married Arwen Valkin (peasant, age 17)
**Age 57 (715 TR)** — His son Durst Corsen married Kelyn Warkin. Durst Corsen married Kelyn Warkin (peasant, age 16)

**Age 58 (716 TR)** — His son Hrolf Corsen married Vala Wheeler. Hrolf Corsen married Vala Wheeler (artisan, age 16)
**Age 58 (716 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Hrolf Corsen's child born (female, Hrolf Corsen age 19)
**Age 59 (717 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
**Age 59 (717 TR)** — One of his children overheard a conversation and formed a conclusion so wrong it took months to correct. By then the wrong version had spread to the neighbours.
**Age 60 (718 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
**Age 60 (718 TR)** — One of his children became convinced that something entirely harmless was dangerous and refused to go near it. The fear lasted two years.

**Age 61 (719 TR)** — He looked around at a feast and realised he knew almost no one his own age. The men he had grown up with, trained with, quarrelled with — most of them were gone. He was still here. He was not sure what that meant.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (675 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Noral Shoemaker (artisan, age 22)
> **Age 19 (676 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.
> **Age 19 (676 TR)** — She bore a child. Wela Shoemaker came into the world healthy. week 40 Wela Shoemaker born (female)
> **Age 20 (677 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 20 (677 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.
> **Age 21 (678 TR)** — She has remade this join four times. The fourth time it is correct by every technical standard she can apply. She is sitting with it and it is still not right and she cannot say why. This is the part of the craft that she cannot explain to anyone who doesn't practise it: the knowledge that something is wrong before she can name what is wrong. She names it eventually, always. She is waiting for the name.
> **Age 21 (678 TR)** — The birth was hard and left her weakened for months. But the child — Amara Shoemaker — lived. week 40 Amara Shoemaker born (female)
> **Age 22 (679 TR)** — She attends the mixed evenings at the guild hall, which are fewer and more formal than the men's evenings, and she has learned to extract what she needs from a different kind of conversation. The women who come are mostly established practitioners who have figured out which parts of the guild structure serve them and which do not. The conversations are more direct. The information is more specific. She gets more from two hours of this than from a full meeting of the general guild council.
> **Age 22 (679 TR)** — Something became funny in a way that built and built until everyone was helpless with it. She remembered it for the rest of her life without being able to say what exactly had been funny.
> **Age 23 (680 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
> **Age 23 (680 TR)** — The birth took longer than expected. Ista Shoemaker lived. She spent the next three days learning who she now was. week 40 Ista Shoemaker born (female)
> **Age 24 (681 TR)** — She grows what she can in the yard and buys the rest from three specific suppliers whose quality she has tested and trusts. The fourth supplier she tried was cheaper and consistently substandard — she tested three batches before concluding the variance was not batch quality but supplier quality. She has a mental map of the supply chain for every material she uses, who she trusts at each point and for what, and what to do when a source fails. This is most of the craft, she has concluded: the material before it reaches your hands.
> **Age 24 (681 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 20
> **Age 25 (682 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
> **Age 25 (682 TR)** — She lost Wela Shoemaker — still a child, not yet grown. Wela Shoemaker died (age 6)
> **Age 26 (683 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
> **Age 27 (684 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
> **Age 27 (684 TR)** — She sat up all night with someone sick. Nothing dramatic happened. By dawn they were through the worst and she went about her day.
> **Age 27 (684 TR)** — She lost Amara Shoemaker — still a child, not yet grown. Amara Shoemaker died (age 6)
> **Age 28 (685 TR)** — She has been waiting for three days for the master to look at the commission design she submitted. The master has been busy. She understands this intellectually. The piece is good — she knows the piece is good — and she is waiting to find out whether knowing it is good and being told it is good are the same thing. They are not the same thing, she has learned. Being told produces a different kind of certainty.
> **Age 28 (685 TR)** — She found something her husband had kept that she had not known about. It was ordinary. She was more curious about why he had kept it than about the thing itself.
> **Age 29 (686 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 29 (686 TR)** — Her first child arrived. Borik Shoemaker. She had not understood, until the moment it was placed in her arms, how much of herself she would give to it. week 40 Borik Shoemaker born (male)
> **Age 29 (686 TR)** — She lost the infant before it had lived. She did not speak of it for a long time. Borik Shoemaker died (age 0)
> **Age 30 (687 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
> **Age 30 (687 TR)** — She bore a child. Yorin Shoemaker came into the world healthy. week 40 Yorin Shoemaker born (male)
> **Age 31 (688 TR)** — The piece was good enough that the mistress put it in the window. This is a specific honour — pieces in the window are examples of what the workshop produces, and only the journeymen's work goes there as a rule. The mistress did not comment on it being apprentice work. She did not need to. The placement said what needed to be said. She has spent considerable time looking at the piece from the street, which she knows is a character flaw, and which she intends to stop once she has made a second piece of equal quality.
> **Age 31 (688 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 32 (689 TR)** — She helped a woman she had never seen carry a load up a hill. The woman tried to pay her; she refused.
> **Age 32 (689 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 33 (690 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
> **Age 34 (691 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
> **Age 34 (691 TR)** — She bore a child. Mael Shoemaker came into the world healthy. week 40 Mael Shoemaker born (male)
> **Age 35 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 35 (692 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
> **Age 35 (692 TR)** — Ista Shoemaker had nearly reached adulthood. She had thought the worst years of worry were behind her. Ista Shoemaker died (age 12)
> **Age 36 (693 TR)** — She came back to herself slowly, and not entirely the same self that had been taken down.
> **Age 36 (693 TR)** — She burned a dinner so completely that it became a family saying. Her spouse ate around it heroically.
> **Age 37 (694 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 38 (695 TR)** — The fever broke on the fourth day. The Esolani who had been attending her — a woman her own age, mild-faced, entirely competent — told her she was past the worst and brought broth. She had not expected to survive. She had a different relationship with the Peonian church after that.
> **Age 38 (695 TR)** — She discovered her husband had a skill she had never known about. She had lived with him for years. She watched him demonstrate it with quiet pride.
> **Age 38 (695 TR)** — She lost Mael Shoemaker — still a child, not yet grown. Mael Shoemaker died (age 4)
> **Age 39 (696 TR)** — She learned something new — weaving, cheese-making, or the basics of leatherwork.
> **Age 40 (697 TR)** — She was sent to collect a pattern from the leatherworkers' guild house and waited while the guild officer found the document. She watched the cutting floor from the window. The leatherworkers had a different relationship with waste than her own trade — they cut for maximum material conservation, which produced a different kind of thinking about the pattern itself. She came back with the document and three observations she has been applying to her own practice.
> **Age 40 (697 TR)** — Her parent told the old story again. She had heard it more times than she could count. She listened anyway and found she was glad of it.
> **Age 41 (698 TR)** — The commission she submitted was assessed as journeyman quality by two of the three guild examiners. The third examiner noted it was produced by a woman and recommended she be assessed against the women's track, which carries different certification than the main guild track and does not lead to a franchise. She asked for a written explanation of which guild statute required this assessment. No written explanation was forthcoming. She submitted a formal complaint to the guild registrar. The complaint is pending, which means it will remain pending until she stops caring or leaves the jurisdiction. She has not decided which she will do.
> **Age 41 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 42 (699 TR)** — She set down her tools and looked at what she had made with quiet satisfaction.
> **Age 43 (700 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
> **Age 44 (701 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back.
> **Age 44 (701 TR)** — Her child overheard part of a conversation and reported it to everyone with complete confidence. The misunderstanding was spectacular.
> **Age 45 (702 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 46 (703 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
> **Age 46 (703 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 47 (704 TR)** — She found someone willing to teach and gave that person her full attention. Studied under Marek Saltworth
> **Age 47 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 48 (705 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 48 (705 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 49 (706 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 49 (706 TR)** — Her child was terrified of something that posed no threat — a goose, a shadow on the wall, a particular sound. She managed the situation with patience and some difficulty.
> **Age 50 (707 TR)** — A fever kept her bedridden for weeks; she emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
> **Age 50 (707 TR)** — Her husband had a habit she had not noticed for years that she suddenly could not unhear. She said nothing. It passed.
> **Age 50 (707 TR)** — Yorin Shoemaker left to make her own way. Yorin Shoemaker left home (age 20)
> **Age 51 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 52 (709 TR)** — She fought for what was hers through every channel available to her.
> **Age 52 (709 TR)** — Her child fell into the water. She was in after them before she thought about it. They were both soaked and frightened and unhurt.
> **Age 53 (710 TR)** — She had known him for years before she realised she would do something difficult for him if he asked. He had, she suspected, reached the same conclusion about her. Marek Saltworth became a close friend
> **Age 53 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 54 (711 TR)** — She mentioned something to one person and by the end of the week it was common knowledge and entirely wrong.
> **Age 55 (712 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Aela Wright died
> **Age 55 (712 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 55 (712 TR)** — Her son Yorin Shoemaker married Leda Karman. Yorin Shoemaker married Leda Karman (peasant, age 19)
> **Age 56 (713 TR)** — She began to doubt the teachings she had followed without question.
> **Age 56 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 57 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 57 (714 TR)** — She and one of her children had grown apart, and then apart further, until there was nothing left.
> **Age 58 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 58 (715 TR)** — She walked a long way with someone she loved without talking much. The quiet was good. She held onto the memory of it.
> **Age 59 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 59 (716 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 60 (717 TR)** — Her long years of service were noticed and formally recognised.
> **Age 61 (718 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 62 (719 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Nora Carter
> **Age 62 (719 TR)** — Her husband was ill for months. She nursed him, managed the household, and kept the worry mostly to herself. He recovered. She had not been sure he would.
> **Age 62 (719 TR)** — She held the grandchild and felt the full weight and joy of years. Yorin Shoemaker's child born (male, Yorin Shoemaker age 32)
> **Age 63 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 63 (720 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
narrative: **Age 18 (675 TR)** — She married. She had opinions about the match that were not the primary consideration in making it. Married Noral Shoemaker (artisan, age 22)

**Age 19 (676 TR)** — She requested reassignment from the guild and they gave it to her, which means the guild agreed that the situation in the first workshop was untenable without either party having to say so directly. The new workshop is better — the master is fair, the work is good, the other journeymen are not the kind of men who make the work worse by being present. She has been here four months and is already producing better work than she was at the previous place. She had not realised how much the atmosphere had been costing her.
**Age 19 (676 TR)** — She bore a child. Wela Shoemaker came into the world healthy. week 40 Wela Shoemaker born (female)

**Age 20 (677 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.

**Age 20 (677 TR)** — He died in the way that men die on the heath — quickly, and with no time given for preparation. She has been continuing since then. Continuing is not the same as being well. It is the thing before being well, if being well eventually comes.

# Aldric Orsen

> [!gm] GM Notes
> **Origin:** Born in a village. Age 28: conscripted, pulled toward the garrison (a town). Age 33: conscripted, pulled toward the garrison (a city).
>
> **Sunsign:** Feneri  |  **Piety:** 15  |  **Born:** 14 Nuzyael
>
> **Archetype: Casual Labourer** — Does whatever work is going — loading carts, clearing rubble, shifting barrels at the docks, digging foundations. No fixed employer. Survives week to week. The most common person in any city.
>
> **Personality (OCEAN):** O:96  C:68  E:2  A:43  N:71
>
> **Morality:** 8 (corruptible)
>
> **Family background:** 1 sibling.
>
> **Deity:** Larani
>
> **Free OPs available:** 24 — allocate before play.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (674 TR)** — He rode out whenever duty permitted. The forest taught him patience and observation.
> **Age 19 (675 TR)** — Shamed by his helplessness, he paid for lessons with a man-at-arms.
> **Age 20 (676 TR)** — The Peonian sister arrived before he had finished sending for her and left after dawn with the kind of professional calm that made everything that had happened in between feel almost ordinary. He does not know her name. He knows the child is alive.
> **Age 21 (677 TR)** — An accident or fight left him with a wound that did not heal cleanly. A serious wound to the chest or torso. It healed, but left a permanent reduction in stamina (−1).
> **Age 22 (678 TR)** — A slow winter and a willing neighbour. He picked up a useful skill.
> **Age 23 (679 TR)** — He made a habit of it — the early mornings, the regular exertion. It accumulated.
> **Age 24 (680 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 25 (681 TR)** — The sky held patterns any farmer learned to read. He had finally learned to read them.
> **Age 26 (682 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
> **Age 27 (683 TR)** — He left in November, which is the worst time to travel and the best time to leave unnoticed — the roads are empty and the lord's reeve does not expect movement. He reached the chartered town in four days and presented himself to the town officer and declared his intent to remain. The year and a day is not a comfortable year. It is a year of knowing that anyone from the home village who recognises you and reports to your lord can end it. He did the work available to him, he stayed within the town boundaries, he did not draw attention. At the end of the year and a day the town officer acknowledged him a freeman. He has never returned to the village. He sends money when he can.
> **Age 28 (684 TR)** — The lord called up the levy. He marched, carrying a spear he barely knew how to hold.
> **Age 29 (685 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Elda Malman (peasant, age 24)
> **Age 30 (686 TR)** — He was ill enough that people began making arrangements. He did not die, which surprised several of them. The illness passed without lasting harm.
> **Age 30 (686 TR)** — He and his spouse had an argument about something so minor he could not remember it two weeks later. The atmosphere lasted much longer.
> **Age 31 (687 TR)** — The campaign was over — or his lord had no further use for him that season. He walked home.
> **Age 31 (687 TR)** — His wife bore twins — Gerin Orsen and Zoric Orsen. The labour was long and hard; she was weakened for months. Both children lived. twins Gerin Orsen and Zoric Orsen born (twins)
> **Age 31 (687 TR)** — The child did not survive its first years. He buried it and said nothing. Zoric Orsen died (age 0)
> **Age 32 (688 TR)** — Someone he knew — not a close friend, but a familiar face from the guild, the village, the neighbourhood — was killed. The world felt less safe after that.
> **Age 33 (689 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 34 (690 TR)** — He recovered by degrees, marking progress in what he could do that he had not managed the week before.
> **Age 34 (690 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
> **Age 35 (691 TR)** — Steel found him. The wound healed, after a fashion.
> **Age 35 (691 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
> **Age 36 (692 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 36 (692 TR)** — Someone in the household was sick through the night. He stayed up. By morning they were better and he was exhausted and quietly relieved.
> **Age 37 (693 TR)** — He travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
> **Age 37 (693 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Geda Orsen came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Geda Orsen born (female)
> **Age 38 (694 TR)** — The court found him guilty. What followed was swift and public.
> **Age 38 (694 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
> **Age 39 (695 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
> **Age 39 (695 TR)** — The child came, and with it something he had not prepared for — an attachment that rearranged everything else. Sarath Orsen. week 40 Sarath Orsen born (female)
> **Age 40 (696 TR)** — A boundary marker, a contested will, a neighbour's greed — the dispute dragged on.
> **Age 40 (696 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 16
> **Age 40 (696 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 41 (697 TR)** — He overheard a conversation he was not meant to hear. He pretended otherwise and spent the next year wondering what to do with it.
> **Age 41 (697 TR)** — His wife found something he had kept from before they met — a letter, a token, an old drawing — and asked about it. He told her the truth. It was not interesting. She had expected it to be interesting.
> **Age 42 (698 TR)** — A friend — soldier, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Deva Borsen died
> **Age 42 (698 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
> **Age 43 (699 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
> **Age 43 (699 TR)** — His wife fell ill — not the brief fever of a week, but something that lingered and frightened. She recovered, mostly. He did not forget the months of it.
> **Age 44 (700 TR)** — The wound had gone bad by the time they brought him to the hospital sisters. He did not remember most of the following week. What he remembered afterward was the smell of herbs and the sound of someone — it might have been the same woman each time — sitting with him in the dark hours and not speaking. He recovered. He did not take this for granted.
> **Age 44 (700 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
> **Age 45 (701 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
> **Age 46 (702 TR)** — The Serolan heard both parties in the chapter house. He was fair in the sense that neither of them got what they wanted. The settlement was binding by custom if not by law and cost him less than a court would have and rather more than he thought the matter was worth.
> **Age 47 (703 TR)** — Time had softened what once seemed unforgivable. They made their peace. Reconciled with Irda Vorkin
> **Age 47 (703 TR)** — She lost the child before its time. There was no husband to grieve beside her. week 20
> **Age 48 (704 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 48 (704 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
> **Age 49 (705 TR)** — He ran the Genin Trail often enough that the same faces appeared twice, then again. Some demanded tolls. Some offered goods — often very good goods, origin unasked. A few just watched from the ridge and let him pass. He learned which behaviour meant which thing.
> **Age 49 (705 TR)** — One of his children said something at the table — a mishearing, a confusion, a small profound mistake — that became the family's private language for years.
> **Age 49 (705 TR)** — His son Gerin Orsen married Calla Idson. Gerin Orsen married Calla Idson (peasant, age 16)
> **Age 50 (706 TR)** — The purse was gone before he reached for it. He had been in the market, in a crowd — a festival, probably, or a hanging-day when the press was thickest. He was annoyed rather than frightened. He knew the city well enough to know this was the cost of moving through it.
> **Age 50 (706 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
> **Age 51 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 51 (707 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
> **Age 52 (708 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 52 (708 TR)** — The child came too still. She had no husband to share the weight of it. week 27
> **Age 52 (708 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Gerin Orsen's child born (male, Gerin Orsen age 21)
> **Age 52 (708 TR)** — Gerin Orsen's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Gerin Orsen's face. Gerin Orsen's child died (age 0)
> **Age 53 (709 TR)** — He recognised something in the young and invested time in it. Mentored Rira Halman
> **Age 54 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 54 (710 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 55 (711 TR)** — Brigands relieved him of coin and dignity alike.
> **Age 55 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 55 (711 TR)** — Geda Orsen left home to make his own way. Geda Orsen left home (age 18)
> **Age 56 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 56 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 56 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 56 (712 TR)** — His daughter Geda Orsen married Harlan Lansen. Geda Orsen married Harlan Lansen (unguilded, age 24)
> **Age 57 (713 TR)** — He tightened his belt and watched his neighbours do the same. Some did not make it.
> **Age 57 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 58 (714 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
> **Age 58 (714 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 59 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 59 (715 TR)** — Gerin Orsen's child Dara Orsen died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Gerin Orsen's child died (age 3)
> **Age 60 (716 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
> **Age 60 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 61 (717 TR)** — He looked around at a feast and realised he knew almost no one his own age. The men he had grown up with, trained with, quarrelled with — most of them were gone. He was still here. He was not sure what that meant.
> **Age 61 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 62 (718 TR)** — The knight stopped him on the road and asked about the sword. It was a shortsword — a simple weapon by every classification in *Manners and Affectations* — and he was a freeman, and carrying a simple weapon is his right. He stated his status clearly and named the section of the arms classification that applied. The knight looked at the sword, looked at him, decided it wasn't worth the argument, and rode on. He stood in the road for a moment afterward noting what he had felt during the exchange, which was primarily a very sharp awareness that the knight's decision could have gone the other way and that the law's protection depends on the armed man deciding to respect it.
> **Age 62 (718 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 63 (719 TR)** — He began to doubt the teachings he had followed without question.
> **Age 63 (719 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 64 (720 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 64 (720 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (673 TR)** — She repaired something — a step, a latch, a cracked fitting — that people had walked past broken for so long they had stopped seeing it.
> **Age 19 (674 TR)** — She came across someone sleeping where they should not have been. She covered them with something and went away.
> **Age 20 (675 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (676 TR)** — Refusing to be a victim again, she found someone willing to teach her.
> **Age 22 (677 TR)** — She walked to the shrine. The journey changed something — not in the way she expected, which was perhaps what it was supposed to do.
> **Age 23 (678 TR)** — She carries a knife. Every woman with any sense carries a knife. A guardsman at the town gate told her knives were not permitted in the market quarter without a licence. She asked him to show her the ordinance. He could not produce it. She waited. He eventually admitted he had misremembered. She thanked him and entered the market quarter. She noted the guardsman's name in her memory. A week later she checked the town charter at the guild house. There is no such ordinance. There never was.
> **Age 24 (679 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark.
> **Age 25 (680 TR)** — The mission had been going well — that was the part that was hardest to hear. Twelve people who thought they understood the situation. She filed it under: the thing you assume is working is the thing that kills you.
> **Age 26 (681 TR)** — She knelt and gave her answers. The Hesa-Eran bound her to the order. She had known since she was twelve it would come to this.
> **Age 27 (682 TR)** — She studied from a written wordlist compiled by someone who had died at Osel, supplemented by conversations with three different informants who disagreed on half the vocabulary. The disagreements were the most informative part. Language is politics in concentrated form.
> **Age 28 (683 TR)** — She ruined two yards of figured cloth that the master had been saving for a specific commission. The sound the mistress made when she saw it was not a word. She sat with that sound for a long time afterward — not out of guilt exactly, but out of the specific determination to understand precisely what had gone wrong at every stage of the decision and execution so that she could guarantee she never made those specific errors again. She can still describe all six errors in sequence. She will never make any of them.
> **Age 29 (684 TR)** — She stood somewhere and saw something beautiful. She kept the image of it longer than most things.
> **Age 30 (685 TR)** — She stayed up until the candle was gone. She was not sure what she had been doing for the last hour.
> **Age 31 (686 TR)** — She was who the village came to when there was no one else.
> **Age 32 (687 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 33 (688 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 34 (689 TR)** — Matakea. The full title. She had spent seven years as Ashesa working toward this and the ceremony itself was over in an afternoon.
> **Age 35 (690 TR)** — Rumour or accusation — fair or unfair — put her standing at risk.
> **Age 36 (691 TR)** — She began writing — not because anyone asked, but because it needed doing.
> **Age 37 (692 TR)** — She stopped at a familiar door and stood on the threshold. She could not have said what made her hesitate. She went in.
> **Age 38 (693 TR)** — Months of close reading: liturgical texts, canonical precedent, the letters of the senior clergy. The work was slow and the illumination on the margins of the oldest manuscripts was occasionally distracting.
> **Age 39 (694 TR)** — A woman she knew was killed — a neighbour, a trader she dealt with, someone whose face she knew. It was the kind of death that made everyone quieter for a while.
> **Age 40 (695 TR)** — She woke knowing what she needed to do. She had gone to sleep with no idea. She did not question it.
> **Age 41 (696 TR)** — A remote posting: a hamlet on the edge of the kingdom, or a lord’s estate where the last priest had left without explanation. The people needed the rites performed and she performed them.
> **Age 42 (697 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
> **Age 43 (698 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 44 (699 TR)** — Patronage came with influence and obligations. She accepted both and managed the latter with care.
> **Age 45 (700 TR)** — She watched the mistress take apart a piece she had thought was finished and rebuild the central section from scratch in forty minutes. It was the same result, technically — the dimensions matched, the specifications were met. But the new version had something the first version had not had, something she could see when they were side by side but cannot yet name precisely. The mistress saw her looking. She did not explain. She said: when you can say what the difference is, you are ready for the examination.
> **Age 46 (701 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 47 (702 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 49 (704 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
> **Age 50 (705 TR)** — She completed the commission and the merchant accepted it and paid the full rate. The merchant did not know she was the apprentice rather than the journeyman. She decided not to clarify this, which is an ethical position she has revisited several times since. Her conclusion: the work was good enough. The classification of who did it is a guild matter, not a customer matter. She told the master. The master said: correct.
> **Age 51 (706 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 52 (707 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Voran
> **Age 53 (708 TR)** — The approach was made in person, by a woman she had never seen, who knew things about her that no one had any business knowing. The price was stated plainly. She paid it. She is still paying it. She has not yet determined who is behind the approach, which tells her they are very good at this.
> **Age 54 (709 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.
> **Age 55 (710 TR)** — She was ill enough to frighten the household. She recovered, though the recovery took longer than the illness. The illness left a lasting mark: stamina −1.
> **Age 56 (711 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
> **Age 57 (712 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 58 (713 TR)** — The Ebasethe arrived before dawn and stayed until the crisis passed. She had sent for her because the midwife said to. She had not expected it to matter. The child was alive. She had a different understanding of the Peonian church after that — less abstract, more personal.
> **Age 59 (714 TR)** — She gave to the church or the poor. It mattered to her that it was done.
> **Age 60 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 61 (716 TR)** — She had managed the household, the accounts, the children's marriages, and the succession of a substantial property for four decades, and she stopped. What she found on the other side of it was time. She had forgotten what time felt like when it belonged to her. She is still learning.
> **Age 62 (717 TR)** — She lit a candle for someone she had not thought of in years. She did not examine this.
> **Age 63 (718 TR)** — Crisis drew her back to her faith with new fervour.
> **Age 64 (719 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 65 (720 TR)** — She set her affairs in order with the same methodical care she had brought to everything. She felt lighter for it.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (672 TR)** — He sweeps the workshop, prepares the materials, cleans the tools, sweeps the workshop again. This is approximately seventy percent of his current working life. The other thirty percent is the actual work, which he is only permitted to do under supervision and at the low end of the skill scale. He is learning to see the difference between the work and the work — between the surface activity and the underlying structure of the craft. He is not certain this perception is accurate. He is fairly certain that the sweeping is not going to stop anytime soon.
> **Age 19 (673 TR)** — A finger was pointed at him — whether justly or not, his name was tainted.
> **Age 20 (674 TR)** — The master struck him across the ear for a piece of work that was not good enough. Not a severe beating — a correction, delivered with the flat of the hand and the specific economy of someone for whom this was a routine instructional tool rather than an expression of anger. The work had not been good enough. He accepted this. He also noted, privately, the specific quality of the master's expression: not angry, not sadistic, but impatient in the way of someone who expected better and was expressing that expectation physically because the verbal expression had not been sufficient. He made the work better. The master did not comment on the improvement.
> **Age 21 (675 TR)** — A fever kept him bedridden for weeks; he emerged gaunt but alive. The illness passed without lasting harm.
> **Age 22 (676 TR)** — The watchman's lantern sweeps across the alley mouth and moves on. He holds himself still for a count of twenty after the footsteps fade before he breathes normally.
> **Age 23 (677 TR)** — He married. The arrangement made sense practically; whether it would make sense in other ways remained to be seen. Married Sirath Orsen (lia_kavair, age 22)
> **Age 23 (677 TR)** — His wife lost the child before its time. He did not know what to do with his grief. week 16
> **Age 24 (678 TR)** — He came home to the door off its hinges and the strongbox open. They had taken the silver plate, his wife's jewellery, and the tools he had been saving for three years. The worst of it was not the loss — it was the knowledge that someone had been through everything, had touched everything, had made themselves at home in his absence.
> **Age 24 (678 TR)** — He discovered that his wife could do something he had never known — sing, draw, whistle like a bird, fix a wheel. He could not say why she had never mentioned it.
> **Age 25 (679 TR)** — The purse is heavier than it looks. He can feel the specific weight of it through the lining of his coat, separate from his own coin, with its own gravity.
> **Age 25 (679 TR)** — She lost the child. He learned the limits of what he could do for her grief, which was most of it. week 17
> **Age 26 (680 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 26 (680 TR)** — He held the infant and understood something new about the world. He had not expected to feel it so immediately. week 40 Gotar Garman born (male)
> **Age 27 (681 TR)** — The mark was permanent — ear, thumb, or cheek. He would carry it the rest of his life.
> **Age 27 (681 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 28 (682 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 28 (682 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Noric Garman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Noric Garman born (male)
> **Age 29 (683 TR)** — He kept company he should not have. Whether it meant anything, only he knew.
> **Age 29 (683 TR)** — His wife bore him a child. Arnam Garman came into the world, and the house changed around that fact. week 40 Arnam Garman born (male)
> **Age 30 (684 TR)** — A merchant or minor lord needed a reliable blade at his back. He fitted the post.
> **Age 30 (684 TR)** — He watched his father — or mother — work through something difficult without complaining. He understood something that he could not have put into words.
> **Age 31 (685 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into his hands.
> **Age 31 (685 TR)** — He and his family took the wrong road on a journey together and were lost for half a day. Everyone had a different idea of where they had gone wrong.
> **Age 32 (686 TR)** — The dice or the wager — it did not matter which. He lost more than he had meant to stake and called it bad luck rather than bad judgment.
> **Age 32 (686 TR)** — His spouse produced a meal so badly wrong that it became a story they told for decades. He ate it without comment.
> **Age 33 (687 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
> **Age 33 (687 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 34 (688 TR)** — The disaster came without warning. What had taken years to build was gone in hours.
> **Age 35 (689 TR)** — He almost missed it — a seam in the floor, a weight in the soil that wasn't right. What he found there changed his year, and perhaps more than his year.
> **Age 36 (690 TR)** — A widowed sister, an aged parent, a cousin with nowhere else to go.
> **Age 36 (690 TR)** — His wife had a habit — a sound she made, a thing she did with her hands, a phrase she repeated — that had not bothered him for years and then, one day, bothered him enormously. He said nothing.
> **Age 37 (691 TR)** — The job was handed to him without explanation — a merchant's strongbox, a specific night, a contact at the eastern gate. He did not know until afterward, when his Shadow named what he had become. There was no ceremony. There were only consequences.
> **Age 38 (692 TR)** — The purse came free clean — three fingers and a thumb — and he was ten strides away before the merchant reached for it. He did not run. Running was what jiggled amateurs. He walked, slowly, until he turned the corner, and then sat down against a wall because his legs had decided to stop working.
> **Age 38 (692 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 39 (693 TR)** — He had trusted the man for four years. The arrest made no sense until he thought back to who had known about the job, and then it made perfect sense. He was out of the city before they came for him — barely. He did not go back for a long time, and when he did, his former colleague was no longer a problem.
> **Age 40 (694 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 40 (694 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 41 (695 TR)** — He found himself navigating allegiances at court that could make or ruin him.
> **Age 41 (695 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 42 (696 TR)** — The fence operates from a chandlery in the river quarter and has been in business for twenty years, which in his line of work means either exceptional competence or exceptional luck, and he is not a lucky-looking man. He takes thirty percent, asks no questions, and moves goods within two days. He pays in coin and has never shorted a payment. He is also the kind of person who would inform on you if the price was right and he judged the arrangement was over. He holds this fact about the fence clearly in mind at all times, which is how you maintain a working relationship with a fence.
> **Age 42 (696 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

> **Age 43 (697 TR)** — He was summoned with the other men of the township to view a body and give verdict to the coroner. The face of the dead stayed with him.
> **Age 43 (697 TR)** — His daughter Helda Garman married Brand Waring. Helda Garman married Brand Waring (lia_kavair, age 21)
> **Age 44 (698 TR)** — He brought his complaint to the constable, who listened with every appearance of attention and did nothing. Three weeks later he learned that the man who had robbed him paid the watch sergeant a monthly retainer. He understood the city better after that. He did not find the understanding comforting.
> **Age 44 (698 TR)** — He walked somewhere with his spouse or a child and they said almost nothing the whole way. It was one of the better journeys he remembered.
