**Age 45 (699 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Noric Garman's illegitimate child born (male, Noric Garman age 17)

**Age 46 (700 TR)** — A killing in the neighbourhood pulled him in — as witness, as kin, as someone who knew both sides. He had to choose where he stood, or at least appear to. Feud with Theric Parman
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — He held the child of his child and understood that time had passed. Gotar Garman's child born (female, Gotar Garman age 20)
**Age 46 (700 TR)** — Helda Garman left home to make his own way. Helda Garman left home (age 19)
**Age 47 (701 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (701 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — He struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Arven Idkin (lia_kavair)
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — One of his children asked him about death — calmly, as children do — and he found he did not have an answer he was happy with.
**Age 48 (702 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Arnam Garman's illegitimate child born (male, Arnam Garman age 19)
**Age 49 (703 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 49 (703 TR)** — The child came still. They had felt it move. He had no words for what followed. week 32
**Age 49 (703 TR)** — Noric Garman's child Rhoric Garman died. He watched his child grieve and could do nothing for either of them. Noric Garman's child died (age 4)
**Age 49 (703 TR)** — His son Yorin Garman married Emra Roring. Yorin Garman married Emra Roring (peasant, age 16)
**Age 50 (704 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 50 (704 TR)** — Something was said — or done — that could not easily be undone. He and his child no longer spoke.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — He arranged for the child to be placed in another household — to learn, to serve, or simply to find a path he could not give them himself. Dalric Garman fostered (age 10)
**Age 51 (705 TR)** — His son Noric Garman married Helra Bransen. Noric Garman married Helra Bransen (lia_kavair, age 18)

**Age 52 (706 TR)** — The territory is not marked on any map. It exists in the knowledge of the people who work it — which streets belong to which shadow, which markets are guild-controlled and which are independent, where the watch changes, which innkeepers pay for advance notice of raids and which call the watch themselves. He spent three months building this map in his head because writing it down would be the kind of thing that could get him killed by either the watch or his own people. He knows it well enough now to navigate by instinct. This is the standard.
**Age 53 (707 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 53 (707 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 53 (707 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 53 (707 TR)** — Yorin Garman left home to make his own way. Yorin Garman left home (age 20)

**Age 54 (708 TR)** — It had begun as professional acquaintance. Somewhere in the course of years it became something else — not sentimentally, but in the way that trust accumulates. Dara Dalman became a close friend
**Age 54 (708 TR)** — Gotar Garman left home to make his own way. Gotar Garman left home (age 28)
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — Time and distance did what deeds could not. The old story lost its hold on people's tongues.
**Age 55 (709 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (710 TR)** — Crisis drew him back to his faith with new fervour.
**Age 56 (710 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 57 (711 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 57 (711 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (712 TR)** — A gamble on goods paid off handsomely.
**Age 58 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (712 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 58 (712 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — Figures were altered, documents forged — profit at the cost of someone else's ruin.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — His son Noric Garman remarried. Noric Garman remarried Dara Butcher (artisan, age 29)
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 59 (713 TR)** — One of his children had a child outside marriage. Dalric Garman's illegitimate child born (female, Dalric Garman age 18)
**Age 60 (714 TR)** — Hunger, debt, or simple greed — he took what was not his.
**Age 60 (714 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (715 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 61 (715 TR)** — His parent told a story he had heard dozens of times as if it were new. He listened as if he had never heard it.
**Age 61 (715 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 61 (715 TR)** — Noric Garman's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Noric Garman's face. Noric Garman's child died (age 2)
**Age 61 (715 TR)** — Dalric Garman's infant died. He grieved for the child and for what he saw in Dalric Garman's face. Dalric Garman's child died (age 2)
**Age 62 (716 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 62 (716 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 63 (717 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.

**Age 63 (717 TR)** — She was ill for three weeks before she died. He had not understood until the second week what the illness was going to become. He had time to understand afterward — the empty space in the work, the meals, the bed — and he understands it still. He is doing what people do. He is continuing.
**Age 63 (717 TR)** — His son Dalric Garman married Edda Branman. Dalric Garman married Edda Branman (lia_kavair, age 16)

**Age 64 (718 TR)** — The constable had a debt he could not pay and a daughter he could not dower. The conversation took place in the back room of a tavern on the river side of the city, and it was brief and businesslike. Afterward, the constable knew which streets to walk slowly on certain nights, and he knew better than to ask why.
**Age 64 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

> [!background] Background
> **Age 18 (671 TR)** — A feud that began with a killing reached into her household. She had no say in the quarrel itself, but the consequences arrived at her door regardless. Feud with Torin Erman
> **Age 19 (672 TR)** — She did not know the woman was wanted. She knew the woman was frightened and had been asking careful questions about the road schedule and had paid a week's lodging in advance, which are each individually unremarkable and together suggest someone who expects to leave quickly. She noted this in her house book without acting on it. When the warrant arrived she cooperated completely with the alderman. She was not prosecuted. She thinks about it sometimes — the specific question of what she owed the woman versus what she owed the town — and has not reached a settled answer.
> **Age 20 (673 TR)** — The mistress cuffed her across the back of the head hard enough to make her ears ring. The reason was a miscounted measure that had ruined two yards of good cloth. She did not cry — she had learned by this point that crying produced worse outcomes than silence. She fixed the count, restarted the section, and did not make the same error again. She has thought about the incident since mostly in terms of what the mistress actually communicated: that the cloth mattered more than the learning, and that the learning was her problem to solve, not the mistress's problem to manage.
> **Age 21 (674 TR)** — She had trained her staff for exactly this eventuality, which she had always suspected was not a matter of if. The evacuation was clean. The loss was significant but not catastrophic: the taproom survived, the brewery survived, only the storage wing and the outer kitchen were gutted. She was trading again within the month. She had not been able to save the guest book, which annoyed her more than the structural damage.
> **Age 22 (675 TR)** — She rode out with hawk or hound when she could. The forest suited her.
> **Age 23 (676 TR)** — The morning begins with whatever the master left unfinished, which is the rule at every workshop and the reason early mornings in every workshop smell the same. She has been doing this for four years. She knows the rhythm of the place — which tasks come in sequence, which the master trusts her with unsupervised, which she is still not permitted to touch. The permitted list grows slowly. She measures her progress by what she is allowed to touch alone.
> **Age 25 (678 TR)** — The master put her hands on the material and showed her how the grain ran under the surface — not visually, by feel. She had been reading grain by eye. Reading it by touch produces different information. The two readings together produce a third kind of knowledge that she did not have before. She has been running her hands over every piece of material she touches since that afternoon, building the vocabulary.
> **Age 26 (679 TR)** — She worked alongside a artisan through a season, watching and helping.
> **Age 29 (682 TR)** — A man of means noticed her. What followed was not love, precisely, but it was not nothing either. She had a room that was warm and food that was regular, and she understood the terms.
> **Age 30 (683 TR)** — He made it legitimate. She had not let herself believe he would. The wedding was modest and the gossip was fierce and she did not care about either.
> **Age 31 (684 TR)** — The other apprentice got the easier tasks, the better hours, the master's patience. She got the corrections and the night work and the jobs that were beneath notice if they went right and humiliating if they went wrong. She understood that this was partly preference and partly a test — the masters who treat their female apprentices harder are testing whether they will leave, because the easy ones always leave and the hard ones stay and become the best practitioners. She stayed. She is still deciding whether the theory justifies the practice.
> **Age 32 (685 TR)** — She said something that was not true when the truth would have served equally well. She was puzzled by herself.
> **Age 33 (686 TR)** — She travelled further from home than ever before, seeing new faces and hearing new tongues.
> **Age 34 (687 TR)** — The lock had been forced cleanly. They had known what they were looking for. Her coin box, the good cloth, the thing her mother had left her. She found herself straightening objects that had been moved and then stopped because she could not stop thinking about the hands that had moved them.
> **Age 35 (688 TR)** — The child lived. She could see from the beginning that they would need more — more care, more patience, more of everything she had. She did not hesitate. week 40 — deaf Karith Morsen born (female, deaf)
> **Age 35 (688 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
> **Age 36 (689 TR)** — She saw a situation developing that she might have done something about. She walked on. The question of whether she was right to has not fully settled.
> **Age 37 (690 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
> **Age 37 (690 TR)** — She caught herself watching the child too long, calculating futures. She stopped herself and went back to work. The watching started again an hour later.
> **Age 38 (691 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.
> **Age 39 (692 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Mira Packworth (merchant)
> **Age 39 (692 TR)** — The child she had carried every fear for stood before her, capable and here. She had been wrong about some of the worst of it. She was glad to have been wrong.
> **Age 40 (693 TR)** — She accompanied a caravan — widow, factor, or simply bold — to distant markets.
> **Age 41 (694 TR)** — She gave to the church or the poor. It mattered to her that it was done.
> **Age 42 (695 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
> **Age 42 (695 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.
> **Age 43 (696 TR)** — The Ebasethe arrived before dawn and stayed until the crisis passed. She had sent for her because the midwife said to. She had not expected it to matter. The child was alive. She had a different understanding of the Peonian church after that — less abstract, more personal.
> **Age 44 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 45 (698 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
> **Age 45 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
> **Age 46 (699 TR)** — The change had been gradual and then sudden. She could trace exactly when it had stopped being repairable. Kern Deepsack became an enemy
> **Age 46 (699 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 47 (700 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
> **Age 47 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 47 (700 TR)** — She lost Karith Morsen — the deaf child she had given so much to. Karith Morsen died (age 12)
> **Age 48 (701 TR)** — She forgot an observance she had kept every year of her adult life. She was not sure what that meant.
> **Age 49 (702 TR)** — She brought the complaint herself, which is her right as a freeholder. The lord's steward had assessed her labour obligation incorrectly and she had the documents to show it. She presented them clearly and without elaboration. The lord looked at the documents and corrected the assessment. She thanked him in the appropriate form and left. The whole proceeding took perhaps half an hour. She noted afterward that the lord had been fair and prompt and had not required her to argue for something the documents already demonstrated. She has a higher opinion of this particular lord than she expected to have.
> **Age 50 (703 TR)** — The Trail was Pagaelin territory in every practical sense regardless of what the maps said. She learned its rhythms — which stretches drew attention, which seasons were dangerous, which small gestures communicated respect without communicating weakness.
> **Age 51 (704 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back.
> **Age 52 (705 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.
> **Age 53 (706 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
> **Age 54 (707 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Kern Deepsack
> **Age 55 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 56 (709 TR)** — She told a story that spread beyond the people she had told it to. She was never sure if that was good.
> **Age 57 (710 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger.
> **Age 58 (711 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
> **Age 59 (712 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
> **Age 60 (713 TR)** — She began writing — not because anyone asked, but because it needed doing.
> **Age 61 (714 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Sera Finecoin
> **Age 62 (715 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
> **Age 63 (716 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.
> **Age 64 (717 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Sarath Plaintrader
> **Age 65 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
> **Age 66 (719 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Halla Rivergate died
> **Age 67 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
> **Age 30** *(class change)* — Married above her station [guilded_innkeeper → merchant]

**Age 35 (688 TR)** — Her child asked her what happened after death. She gave the best answer she had. She was not sure it was good enough.
**Age 36 (689 TR)** — She saw a situation developing that she might have done something about. She walked on. The question of whether she was right to has not fully settled.

**Age 37 (690 TR)** — She heard the cry and had the choice that women always have in these moments: whether joining would help or whether she would be more useful as a witness, which is a calculation she makes in approximately half a second. She positioned herself where she could see the man being pursued, watched where he went, and when the officers arrived she described his route and his description clearly and they found him two streets over. She was thanked by the bailiff. She has been a useful witness in two similar situations. She considers this a form of civic participation she is good at.
**Age 37 (690 TR)** — She caught herself watching the child too long, calculating futures. She stopped herself and went back to work. The watching started again an hour later.
**Age 38 (691 TR)** — The death of a relative brought unexpected property or coin into her hands.
**Age 39 (692 TR)** — She struck up an acquaintance that would prove valuable in years to come. Made contact: Mira Packworth (merchant)
**Age 39 (692 TR)** — The child she had carried every fear for stood before her, capable and here. She had been wrong about some of the worst of it. She was glad to have been wrong.
**Age 40 (693 TR)** — She accompanied a caravan — widow, factor, or simply bold — to distant markets.
**Age 41 (694 TR)** — She gave to the church or the poor. It mattered to her that it was done.

**Age 42 (695 TR)** — She noticed the lightness at her belt on the way home and knew. A market crowd, a moment of distraction. She had been careful. It had not been enough. She counted what she still had left and did not dwell on it.
**Age 42 (695 TR)** — Her child produced a phrase so perfectly wrong that the whole household adopted it. Visitors were puzzled. The family didn't explain.

**Age 43 (696 TR)** — The Ebasethe arrived before dawn and stayed until the crisis passed. She had sent for her because the midwife said to. She had not expected it to matter. The child was alive. She had a different understanding of the Peonian church after that — less abstract, more personal.
**Age 44 (697 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 45 (698 TR)** — Her childbearing years had passed. She felt the change in herself, and adapted.
**Age 45 (698 TR)** — The year passed much as the last had.
**Age 46 (699 TR)** — The change had been gradual and then sudden. She could trace exactly when it had stopped being repairable. Kern Deepsack became an enemy
**Age 46 (699 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.

**Age 47 (700 TR)** — Serving on the assize jury is not common for women but her holding qualifies her and the bailiff called her. She went. She listened more carefully than most jurors she observed, because she was aware that her presence was being watched by everyone in the room and that any appearance of confusion would be attributed to her sex rather than to the complexity of the case. The cases were not complex. She returned a clear verdict in each. The bailiff thanked her with the specific tone of a man who was expecting difficulty and didn't get it.
**Age 47 (700 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 47 (700 TR)** — She lost Karith Morsen — the deaf child she had given so much to. Karith Morsen died (age 12)
**Age 48 (701 TR)** — She forgot an observance she had kept every year of her adult life. She was not sure what that meant.

**Age 49 (702 TR)** — She brought the complaint herself, which is her right as a freeholder. The lord's steward had assessed her labour obligation incorrectly and she had the documents to show it. She presented them clearly and without elaboration. The lord looked at the documents and corrected the assessment. She thanked him in the appropriate form and left. The whole proceeding took perhaps half an hour. She noted afterward that the lord had been fair and prompt and had not required her to argue for something the documents already demonstrated. She has a higher opinion of this particular lord than she expected to have.

**Age 50 (703 TR)** — The Trail was Pagaelin territory in every practical sense regardless of what the maps said. She learned its rhythms — which stretches drew attention, which seasons were dangerous, which small gestures communicated respect without communicating weakness.
**Age 51 (704 TR)** — She handed the accounts and keys to a younger hand and stepped back.
**Age 52 (705 TR)** — She went somewhere she had never been, which was unusual enough that she paid careful attention the entire time.

**Age 53 (706 TR)** — The franchise had come available when old Maret died with no heirs. She had moved faster than the men who assumed she would not, and had the backing of two syndics before the others had finished shaking their heads. The vote was closer than she liked. She did not let this show.
**Age 54 (707 TR)** — She chose to end the enmity. It cost her pride and gained her something she could not name. Reconciled with Kern Deepsack
**Age 55 (708 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 56 (709 TR)** — She told a story that spread beyond the people she had told it to. She was never sure if that was good.

**Age 57 (710 TR)** — She would not have known him except that he laughed — the same short laugh, the kind that means nothing is funny. The cutpurse from the Endakil festival. She watched him move through the crowd with professional ease and felt something cold and very precise in her chest that was not exactly anger.

**Age 58 (711 TR)** — She is here as a witness, not a party. A woman from the village is contesting her status as a freeholder against the new lord's claim that she is a serf. She knows this woman. She knows the family's history on that land going back two generations. She has been asked to testify to what she knows. She will say exactly what she knows and nothing she doesn't know and she will be as clear as possible about which is which. The moot's ability to get this right depends on witnesses being honest. She intends to be an example of what that looks like.
**Age 59 (712 TR)** — The recovery was long. She measured it in small returns — the walk she could finish, the task she could complete.
**Age 60 (713 TR)** — She began writing — not because anyone asked, but because it needed doing.
**Age 61 (714 TR)** — She took a younger woman under her wing and passed on what she knew. Mentored Sera Finecoin
**Age 62 (715 TR)** — She was called upon to speak where women rarely did — and was not found wanting.
**Age 63 (716 TR)** — She let go of what she could no longer do and found she still had plenty left.
**Age 64 (717 TR)** — A secret came to her unbidden. Keeping it was both burden and power. Confided in Sarath Plaintrader
**Age 65 (718 TR)** — Another difficult year. Life continued.
**Age 66 (719 TR)** — A friend — companion, neighbour, or kin — died badly. The loss left a mark. Halla Rivergate died
**Age 67 (720 TR)** — He endured. The details were different; the shape was the same.
**Age 30** *(class change)* — Married above her station [guilded_innkeeper → merchant]
